questions.q: An AI model for customer service routing has been deployed for 18 months. The operations team reports that the categories of customer queries have changed significantly since training. What should be assessed?
opts: Whether the model's serving infrastructure needs upgrading after 18 months of continuous operation
Whether the model's category definitions still match current business categories and whether retraining or redesign is needed
Whether 18 months of operation justifies transitioning the model from project to IT maintenance
Whether the monitoring alerts need to be recalibrated based on 18 months of observed performance data
questions.explain: Significantly changed query categories after 18 months indicate potential model obsolescence — the categories the model was trained to classify may no longer match what customers are actually asking. This requires assessing whether the current model's category schema still reflects business reality and whether retraining (with updated categories) or complete redesign is needed. Infrastructure age and monitoring calibration are secondary.

questions.q: After an AI project closes, the project manager discovers the model was never added to the organization's AI model inventory. What is the risk of this omission?
opts: The organization lacks visibility and governance over the model — it cannot be tracked for compliance, monitored systematically, or included in risk assessments
The model may be incorrectly attributed to a different project in future annual AI portfolio reviews
The data science team may not receive credit for the model in performance evaluations
The model may not receive scheduled infrastructure updates managed by the IT operations team
questions.explain: An AI model not in the organization's inventory is invisible to governance — it can't be included in compliance audits, systematically monitored for drift or bias, assessed in organizational risk reviews, or properly decommissioned when needed. CPMAI requires adding all deployed AI models to a model registry/inventory as a fundamental governance practice at project closure.

questions.q: A deployed AI model for inventory optimization begins producing recommendations that operations staff consistently override. What should the project manager investigate FIRST?
opts: Whether the consistent overrides indicate the model's outputs are misaligned with actual operational constraints or business rules
Whether the override rate exceeds the threshold defined in the monitoring plan for triggering retraining
Whether operations staff received adequate training on how to interpret model recommendations
Whether the model's recommendations are technically correct based on its training objectives
questions.explain: Consistent user overrides are a signal that the model's recommendations don't match operational reality — not primarily a training problem or a threshold issue. CPMAI requires investigating whether the model is misaligned with actual business rules, operational constraints, or user knowledge. If users consistently know better, the model needs to incorporate what they know.

questions.q: What is the MAIN reason to establish human override capability for AI-generated decisions in a deployed system?
opts: To collect feedback data from human overrides to improve model performance in future retraining cycles
To reduce compute costs by allowing humans to handle cases where the model's confidence is below threshold
To comply with organizational policies requiring all technology systems to have a manual bypass option
To maintain human accountability and correct AI errors in consequential decisions — humans must retain ultimate authority
questions.explain: CPMAI's accountability principle requires humans to retain ultimate authority over consequential AI decisions. Override capability ensures that when the AI is wrong, a human can correct the error before it causes harm — and that the human, not the AI, is accountable for the final decision. Compliance and feedback collection are secondary purposes; cost reduction is not a primary rationale.

sections.title: AI Project Management Scenarios
sections.description: Stakeholder management · Scope · Risk · Schedule · Team · Communication · Governance

questions.q: A project manager is asked to provide a status update to the steering committee on an AI project in Phase III. The model is behind schedule. What should the project manager present?
opts: Current model accuracy metrics from the most recent training run and the target accuracy for comparison
Honest status with root cause of delay, impact on overall project timeline, proposed recovery options, and any decisions needed from the committee
A revised timeline only, with no root cause analysis to avoid creating unnecessary alarm
Confirmation that the team is working hard and confidence that the schedule will be recovered without steering committee intervention
questions.explain: Steering committee updates require honest status, root cause transparency, and clear options for the committee to consider. CPMAI and PMI project management both require complete, accurate reporting — not optimistic framing or avoiding root cause to manage impressions. The steering committee needs facts and options, not reassurances, to make informed governance decisions.

questions.q: An AI project's executive sponsor is replaced halfway through Phase III. The new sponsor is skeptical about AI and wants a detailed briefing. What should the project manager present first?
opts: The project's business case, Phase I feasibility findings, and current progress against success criteria
A comparison of the project's AI approach against competitor implementations in the same industry
The budget spent to date and the projected costs to complete development and deployment
The technical model architecture and algorithm selection rationale for the current development sprint
questions.explain: A skeptical new sponsor needs the business context first — why this AI project was approved, what business problem it solves, what the feasibility assessment determined, and how current progress tracks against success criteria. Technical architecture details, competitor comparisons, and budget details are secondary supporting information after the business rationale is established.

questions.q: A project manager discovers that two team members — a data scientist and a compliance officer — have fundamental disagreements about whether a planned model feature is acceptable. The issue has stalled progress for two weeks. What should the project manager do?
opts: Allow more time for the parties to reach consensus since forcing a resolution may damage team relationships
Adopt the compliance officer's position as the default since regulatory risk outweighs performance gains
Side with the data scientist since technical accuracy must take precedence in model development
Escalate the disagreement to the steering committee for a decision since both parties have legitimate authority in their domains
questions.explain: A two-week standoff between a data scientist (technical authority) and a compliance officer (regulatory authority) both with legitimate domain expertise is a governance issue requiring escalation. The steering committee or appropriate decision authority can make the binding decision with full visibility into both perspectives. Allowing indefinite standoff or defaulting to one party's position without governance review is not appropriate.

questions.q: An AI project is entering Phase V operationalization. The business team identifies 15 additional features they want before going live. What should the project manager recommend?
opts: Accept the scope change only if the data science team can complete the features without extending the timeline
Implement all 15 features to maximize business value before the first production release
Prioritize the 15 features with the business team, deliver the highest-value features in the initial release, and plan subsequent releases for the remainder
Defer all 15 features to post-launch since scope changes at Phase V are never appropriate
questions.explain: 15 new features at Phase V requires scope negotiation — delivering everything delays launch and adds risk; delivering nothing leaves business value on the table. CPMAI project management involves prioritizing features with the business team based on value and readiness, delivering the most critical in the initial release, and planning subsequent releases for the remainder. This is phased delivery aligned to stakeholder priorities.

questions.q: A project sponsor pressures the project manager to skip the Phase I feasibility assessment to accelerate delivery. What is the MOST important reason to resist this pressure?
opts: Skipping Phase I violates PMI's standard project management code of ethics
Phase I feasibility assessment is the foundation for all subsequent phases — proceeding without it risks delivering a technically complete but strategically wrong solution
The project will fail the post-deployment review if Phase I documentation is absent
Phase I is the only opportunity to obtain cross-functional stakeholder alignment on project scope
questions.explain: Phase I feasibility is foundational — it establishes whether AI is the right approach, whether data is available, whether the problem is framed correctly, and what success looks like. Skipping it to accelerate timeline risks delivering a technically functional but strategically misaligned solution. Other phases cannot compensate for missing Phase I foundations.

questions.q: A stakeholder requests that the AI project team ensure 'the model never makes a mistake.' How should the project manager respond?
opts: Set the highest achievable accuracy as the target and define clear error rate SLAs for acceptable performance
Clarify that no AI model achieves 100% accuracy and collaboratively define acceptable error thresholds aligned to business risk tolerance
Explain that the human review layer after model output effectively eliminates errors from reaching decisions
Agree and establish a 100% accuracy target as the project success criterion
questions.explain: 100% accuracy is technically impossible for any real-world AI system. The project manager must clarify this reality and collaboratively establish acceptable error thresholds based on business risk tolerance — what type of errors are acceptable, at what rate, and what the consequences of errors are. This is a foundational success criteria conversation required in Phase I.

questions.q: An AI project team includes data scientists, data engineers, and business analysts. The data scientists consistently make technical decisions without consulting the business analysts. What should the project manager address?
opts: Transfer the business analysts to a separate advisory role to prevent conflict with the technical team
Accept the dynamic since data scientists should have final authority on technical decisions
Assign the business analyst team a separate reporting line to protect their independence
Establish a cross-functional review cadence where technical decisions are validated against business requirements with business analyst input
questions.explain: AI project teams require cross-functional collaboration between technical and business members at all decision points. Technical decisions made without business validation frequently miss business requirements, leading to models that are technically elegant but operationally irrelevant. CPMAI emphasizes structured collaboration through review cadences, not organizational separation or technical dominance.

questions.q: The project manager of an AI project is asked by legal to confirm that all training data sources have documented consent and terms of use compliance. What should the project manager verify in Phase II documentation?
opts: That a senior data scientist reviewed all data sources for technical suitability before training began
That the data engineering team signed off on data pipeline architecture covering all training data sources
That data consent and terms of use were evaluated for each source in the data requirements and inventory documentation
That the model card lists all training data sources by name for legal reference
questions.explain: Legal requires confirmation that data consent and terms of use compliance were evaluated — this should be documented in Phase II data requirements and inventory documentation. CPMAI Phase II includes evaluating legal rights to use each data source as a required step. If this isn't in the Phase II documentation, it may not have been done — and the project manager needs to address that gap before confirming compliance to legal.

questions.q: The business stakeholders request a significant new feature for an AI model that is already in Phase IV model evaluation. What should the project manager do?
opts: Implement the feature immediately since business stakeholder requests represent requirements
Assess the impact of the change request on timeline, data requirements, and evaluation plans before accepting or deferring it
Accept the feature only if the data science team confirms it can be added without additional training time
Defer all change requests until after deployment and maintain the current scope for Phase IV
questions.explain: A significant new feature request in Phase IV triggers a change assessment: what data does the new feature require (Phase II impact), how does it change training requirements (Phase III impact), and how does it affect the evaluation plan (Phase IV impact)? This may require returning to an earlier phase. CPMAI requires impact assessment before accepting, deferring, or declining any scope change.

questions.q: A data scientist proposes a technically advanced approach that the project manager believes the business cannot operationalize given current capabilities. What should the project manager do?
opts: Collaborate with the data scientist and operations stakeholders to evaluate technical approaches against operational feasibility
Require the data scientist to select a simpler approach that can be operationalized within current capabilities
Present the advanced approach to the sponsor and let leadership decide based on strategic AI ambitions
Approve the technical approach and address operationalization challenges during Phase V
questions.explain: CPMAI requires evaluating technical approaches against operational feasibility — the best technical solution that cannot be operationalized is not the best solution for the business. Collaboration between data science and operations to find an approach that is both technically sound and operationally feasible is the correct project management action.

questions.q: A project manager is managing an AI project where the data science team reports that each iteration takes 3 weeks and the scope requires 15 iterations. How should the project manager handle this?
opts: Reduce the number of model features to accelerate each iteration and meet a shorter timeline
Replace the current development approach with AutoML tools to reduce iteration time to one week
Accept the timeline of 45 weeks since AI projects are inherently iterative and cannot be compressed
Evaluate which iterations can be parallelized, which can be eliminated based on prioritization, and develop a phased delivery plan with the sponsor
questions.explain: CPMAI project management requires proactive timeline management — evaluating parallelization opportunities, prioritizing the highest-value iterations, and working with the sponsor to define a phased delivery plan that delivers value progressively rather than waiting 45 weeks for a single delivery. Accepting the timeline without optimization or compressing quality are both unsatisfactory responses.

questions.q: A project manager is facilitating a Phase IV model evaluation review. The data science team presents results showing the model meets technical criteria. The operations team representative says they haven't had time to evaluate operational readiness. What should the project manager do?
opts: Allow the technical sign-off to proceed and make operations review a post-deployment condition
Close the review meeting and declare the model ready for deployment based on technical criteria
Reschedule the review to allow the operations team adequate time to complete their evaluation
Proceed with deployment planning while operations completes their review in parallel
questions.explain: Model evaluation go/no-go requires all designated reviewers to complete their evaluations — not just the technical team. Operational readiness (deployment infrastructure, monitoring plan, incident procedures, user training) is a mandatory evaluation domain. Proceeding without operations sign-off deploys a technically evaluated but operationally unprepared model. Reschedule to ensure complete cross-functional review.

questions.q: An AI project is delivered on schedule and within budget but post-deployment results show it is not used by the intended end users. What is MOST likely the root cause?
opts: The model's accuracy was insufficient to meet end user quality expectations for daily use
The project team failed to include end user requirements, workflows, and adoption planning in the project scope
The deployment timeline was too aggressive, preventing adequate user testing before go-live
The project sponsor failed to communicate the mandate for AI tool adoption to the affected teams
questions.explain: On-time, on-budget delivery with zero adoption is a classic project management failure: technical success without user-centered design. CPMAI requires including end user requirements, workflow integration design, and adoption planning as explicit project scope elements — not afterthoughts. When these are omitted, technically correct solutions solve the wrong user problem.

questions.q: During an AI project, the project manager learns that two similar AI initiatives are running in different business units without coordination. What is the MOST appropriate action?
opts: Escalate to program management or governance to coordinate the initiatives, prevent duplication, and identify sharing opportunities
Notify the other project manager and informally align approaches between the two teams
Request that one of the two projects be cancelled since duplicate AI initiatives waste organizational resources
Continue managing the current project independently since cross-unit coordination is outside the project scope
questions.explain: Duplicate AI initiatives represent governance and resource efficiency issues that should be escalated to program management or AI governance — not ignored, informally coordinated, or unilaterally cancelled. Governance bodies can evaluate whether to merge, coordinate, or differentiate the initiatives based on full organizational context. CPMAI project managers are responsible for flagging these issues to the appropriate level.

questions.q: An AI project risk register identifies 'insufficient labeled training data' as a high-probability, high-impact risk. What is the BEST risk response?
opts: Transfer the risk to the data engineering team since labeling is their operational responsibility
Avoid the risk by selecting an unsupervised learning approach that doesn't require labeled data
Accept the risk and address it if it materializes during Phase III model development
Mitigate the risk by planning data labeling activities and resources in Phase II before development begins
questions.explain: A high-probability, high-impact risk requires active mitigation before it materializes. Planning and resourcing data labeling in Phase II — before development depends on labeled data — is the CPMAI-aligned response. Accepting a high-probability high-impact risk, assigning it to another team without resources, or changing the AI approach to avoid the problem are all less effective responses.

questions.q: A team member informs the project manager that the model's training data includes records from customers who explicitly opted out of data-sharing. What should the project manager do IMMEDIATELY?
opts: Document the issue in the project risk register and continue development while the legal team investigates
Remove the opt-out records from training data and retrain the model to correct the issue
Notify legal, pause any further use of the compromised training data, and initiate a formal data audit to determine scope
Assess whether removing opt-out records would materially affect model performance before deciding on action
questions.explain: Using data from individuals who explicitly opted out is a privacy and legal violation requiring immediate escalation — not a change management decision, a performance assessment, or a risk register entry. CPMAI requires: immediate notification of legal, halting further use of the compromised data, and a formal audit to understand the full scope of the violation. Development cannot continue with data known to violate privacy commitments.

questions.q: After the AI project is complete and the model is in production, the project manager receives a message that a downstream system consuming model outputs has changed its input format without notifying the AI team. Model predictions are now being ignored by that system. What should the project manager ensure is in place for future cases?
opts: A technical dependency map documenting all systems that consume model outputs and a change notification process requiring advance notice before integrations change
A quarterly audit of all downstream systems to verify they are correctly consuming model outputs
A technical escalation process for the AI team to notify the IT steering committee when integrations break
An API versioning strategy that allows the model to serve multiple output formats simultaneously
questions.explain: This is a governance and integration management failure: downstream systems changed without notifying the model team. The prevention is a dependency registry (documenting all consuming systems) and a change management process requiring consuming systems to notify the AI team before changing integration contracts. API versioning helps manage changes gracefully once the governance process is in place.

questions.q: What distinguishes successful AI project management from traditional IT project management?
opts: AI projects are inherently experimental and iterative — success requires embracing uncertainty, managing data as a core constraint, and defining success in terms of business outcomes rather than feature delivery
AI projects use different project management methodologies that are incompatible with PMI standards
AI projects always require more stakeholder communication than IT projects due to higher public interest in AI
AI projects require larger teams and longer timelines than equivalent IT projects
questions.explain: AI projects differ fundamentally: they are experimental (the model may not work as expected), iterative (multiple training cycles are normal), data-dependent (data availability and quality are primary constraints), and outcome-focused (success is measured by business impact, not feature delivery). CPMAI adapts project management to these characteristics — embracing uncertainty and iteration rather than fighting them.

questions.q: A project team building an AI model for financial risk assessment asks if they need to document assumptions made during feature engineering. What should the project manager say?
opts: Documentation of assumptions is only required for assumptions that affect model accuracy, not those affecting data preparation
Assumptions documentation is required only if the model will be reviewed by external regulators or auditors
Documentation is optional for technical decisions made by subject matter experts using professional judgment
Yes — assumptions in feature engineering affect model validity and must be documented for audit, reproducibility, and future model maintenance
questions.explain: Feature engineering assumptions — why a variable was transformed a specific way, why certain records were excluded, why a particular encoding was chosen — affect model validity and must be documented. Without documentation, the model cannot be audited, reproduced, or maintained by future teams. CPMAI requires documenting all material assumptions throughout the development process, not just for regulatory audiences.

questions.q: During a sprint review, the data science team presents a model that achieves the technical accuracy target but uses a feature the legal team has flagged as a privacy concern. What should the project manager do?
opts: Reduce the weight of the flagged feature to minimize its influence while maintaining accuracy
Proceed with the model since technical accuracy targets were met and legal will review at deployment
Pause development and resolve the legal concern before the model proceeds to further development
Replace the feature with a proxy variable that achieves similar accuracy without the direct privacy concern
questions.explain: A legally flagged privacy concern must be resolved before the model proceeds further — not at deployment. Proceeding wastes additional development effort on a model with a known compliance blocker. Proxy variables may raise their own privacy concerns and require legal review. Reducing weight doesn't remove the legal concern. CPMAI requires addressing legal and ethical issues when identified, not deferring them.

sections.title: Domain I: Responsible & Trustworthy AI
sections.description: Governance · Bias · Fairness · Privacy · Transparency · Explainability · Oversight

questions.q: An AI model is deployed to screen job applications. Six months later, an internal audit finds male applicants are shortlisted at twice the rate of equally qualified female applicants. What is the FIRST action?
opts: Retrain the model immediately with a balanced dataset and redeploy within two weeks
Suspend the model, notify affected candidates, and initiate a formal bias investigation
Adjust the shortlisting threshold for female applicants to equalize outcomes
Document the finding as a known limitation and add a disclaimer to model outputs
questions.explain: A confirmed discriminatory bias in a deployed hiring model requires immediate harm containment: suspend the model to stop ongoing harm, notify affected candidates (accountability and transparency), and investigate root cause. Retraining without investigation risks repeating the bias. Threshold adjustment treats symptoms. Documentation without action is insufficient.

questions.q: The operations team requests that all AI model decisions be logged permanently for audit purposes. The data science team says permanent logging is infeasible due to storage costs. What should the project manager do?
opts: Work with legal, compliance, and operations to define a retention period that satisfies audit requirements without unlimited storage
Accept the operations requirement and identify budget for additional storage infrastructure
Defer the decision to post-deployment and implement basic logging initially
Align with the data science team since storage costs justify limiting the logging retention period
questions.explain: Neither the operations team's 'permanent' requirement nor the data science team's 'infeasible' position is the project manager's endpoint. The right action is facilitation: bring legal and compliance into the conversation to define a retention period that satisfies regulatory and audit requirements within practical constraints. CPMAI project managers mediate competing stakeholder requirements, not pick sides.

questions.q: The data science team proposes a model that achieves 97% accuracy but cannot explain which inputs drive specific decisions. The use case is automated loan denials. What should the project manager recommend?
opts: Deploy with an appeals process so denied applicants can request manual review
Add a standard explanation template to all denials that describes the model's general criteria
Deploy the model since 97% accuracy substantially reduces manual review workload and errors
Require interpretability tools or select a more explainable model given the regulatory requirement to explain adverse credit decisions
questions.explain: Automated loan denials require explaining adverse decisions to applicants — a legal requirement under regulations like ECOA (Equal Credit Opportunity Act). An unexplainable black-box model cannot satisfy this requirement. CPMAI requires interpretability tools or an inherently explainable model for regulated, consequential decisions, regardless of accuracy.

questions.q: An AI system provides medical imaging analysis support to radiologists. After deployment, a radiologist notices the system consistently underperforms on images from patients over 80 years old. What should the project manager do?
opts: Train the radiologist team to apply additional scrutiny to elderly patient outputs as a compensating control
Log the observation for the next scheduled model review cycle in three months
Immediately escalate the finding as a patient safety risk and assess whether the model should be restricted until remediated
Increase the confidence threshold for elderly patient images to reduce false outputs
questions.explain: Systematic underperformance on a specific patient group in a medical imaging application is a patient safety risk — not a scheduled review item. CPMAI requires immediate escalation and assessment of whether the model should be restricted or suspended until the bias is investigated and remediated. Safety-critical bias cannot be managed through thresholds or user training alone.

questions.q: During a post-deployment audit, an external reviewer finds the project team cannot produce documentation showing how training data was selected. What responsible AI principle does this gap most directly violate?
opts: Fairness — without selection documentation, disparate impact cannot be demonstrated or refuted
Transparency and accountability — the organization cannot explain how the AI system was built or be held accountable for its design choices
Privacy — data selection documentation is required to verify consent compliance for all training records
Reliability — undocumented data selection cannot be reproduced for model retraining
questions.explain: Inability to explain how training data was selected is a transparency and accountability failure — the organization cannot account for its AI design choices to auditors, regulators, or affected parties. While this may also affect fairness assessments, privacy verification, and reproducibility, the primary principle violated is transparency and accountability in the development process.

questions.q: A model's output is used by customer service agents who sometimes override it based on their own judgment. The project manager notices override rates are much higher for certain customer demographics. What does this pattern MOST likely indicate?
opts: The model may be underperforming for those demographics, leading agents to distrust its outputs for those groups
Agents are demonstrating personal bias by overriding model decisions for specific customer types
The model confidence scores are not calibrated to reflect actual uncertainty for different demographics
Customer service agents are poorly trained on how to interpret model outputs for edge cases
questions.explain: Disproportionately high override rates for specific demographics is a signal that the model is underperforming for those groups — agents override because they recognize the outputs are wrong. This is a bias and fairness signal requiring investigation, not primarily a training, agent behavior, or calibration problem. CPMAI requires treating override patterns as a model quality feedback signal.

questions.q: The legal team informs the project manager that the AI model may be subject to a new AI regulation requiring human oversight of automated decisions. The model is currently fully automated. What should the project manager do?
opts: Add a disclosure statement to model outputs indicating the decision was AI-generated
Reduce the volume of automated decisions until the legal team issues a final determination
Confirm the regulation's applicability and add a human review checkpoint before acting on any model-generated decision
Continue operating while the legal team determines whether the regulation applies to this specific model
questions.explain: When a regulation potentially requiring human oversight is identified, the risk-adjusted correct action is to implement human oversight immediately — before a definitive legal determination — rather than continuing to operate in possible violation. Adding disclosure or reducing volume without adding oversight doesn't satisfy a human oversight requirement. Waiting for legal determination while continuing full automation is the highest-risk posture.

questions.q: During model development, a team member suggests using customers' social media activity to improve churn prediction accuracy. The project manager should evaluate FIRST:
opts: Whether the data engineering pipeline can process social media data within training time constraints
Whether the social media data correlates strongly enough with churn to justify the integration effort
Whether customers consented to their social media data being used for commercial churn prediction
Whether a data vendor can supply social media data at a cost within the project budget
questions.explain: Social media data use for commercial prediction raises consent and ethical use questions before technical evaluation. Users likely did not consent to their social media activity being used for churn prediction when they created accounts. CPMAI requires evaluating legal and ethical appropriateness first — consent and purpose limitation take priority over correlation strength, cost, or pipeline capacity.

questions.q: A project team completes development of an AI model that predicts recidivism risk for parole decisions. Before presenting results to the governance board, what should the project manager ensure is included?
opts: Model accuracy benchmarks comparing performance to human parole board decisions
Bias analysis across racial and demographic groups with documented mitigation steps
A cost-benefit analysis showing budget savings from AI-assisted parole reviews
Infrastructure specifications for scaling the model to all correctional facilities
questions.explain: Recidivism prediction is a high-stakes, high-bias-risk application with documented history of racial disparity. Before governance review, the project manager must ensure bias analysis across demographic groups with documented mitigation is completed. Accuracy benchmarks, cost savings, and scaling specs are secondary to responsible AI validation for this use case.

questions.q: An AI project team is asked to add a 'human in the loop' review step for all model outputs before they affect customer accounts. A developer argues this eliminates the efficiency gain of AI. How should the project manager respond?
opts: Explain that human oversight is a governance requirement for consequential decisions and the efficiency case should be made within that constraint, not against it
Implement human review for the first six months and remove it once the model establishes a track record
Reduce the scope of model outputs requiring review to the lowest-risk transaction types only
Agree and propose removing the human review step for high-confidence predictions above a threshold
questions.explain: Human oversight for consequential AI decisions is a governance principle — not a variable to optimize around efficiency gains. CPMAI requires the project manager to explain this clearly: the efficiency case should be built within governance constraints (e.g., optimizing the review process), not by arguing against the oversight requirement itself.

questions.q: An AI content recommendation system is found to reinforce users' existing viewpoints by showing only content aligned with their prior engagement. What type of responsible AI concern does this represent?
opts: A model accuracy problem — the recommendations don't match users' full range of interests
A privacy concern — the model processes user behavior without explicit content preference consent
A data quality issue — the training data lacks diversity in content types and engagement signals
A feedback loop bias creating filter bubbles — the model optimizes for engagement without regard to information diversity or societal impact
questions.explain: A recommendation system that reinforces existing viewpoints creates feedback loop bias and filter bubbles — it optimizes the engagement metric while narrowing information exposure over time. This is a responsible AI concern about societal impact beyond individual user interactions. It differs from data quality, accuracy, and privacy issues.

questions.q: A project manager is told the AI bias testing plan will take three additional weeks, pushing the launch date. The sponsor asks if bias testing can be done after launch on live data. What is the BEST response?
opts: Present the three-week delay as a project risk for the sponsor to accept or mitigate with additional resources
Agree since post-launch testing on real data will be more representative than pre-launch testing
Explain that launching without bias testing exposes real users to potential discriminatory outcomes that should be validated before deployment
Agree to a compressed two-week bias test while planning comprehensive testing post-launch
questions.explain: Bias testing must be completed before launch — not after. Post-launch bias testing on live data means real users are exposed to potential discriminatory outcomes while testing occurs. CPMAI responsible AI principles require bias validation before any user impact. The project manager's obligation is to explain this clearly, not to defer pre-launch safety testing to accommodate schedule pressure.

questions.q: A team proposes using a synthetic dataset generated by AI to augment limited training data for a credit scoring model. What should the project manager evaluate BEFORE approving this approach?
opts: Whether synthetic data generation can be completed within the remaining Phase II timeline
Whether the synthetic data preserves the statistical properties of real data without introducing artifacts or amplifying existing biases
Whether the synthetic data approach reduces the storage requirements compared to collecting additional real data
Whether the model's accuracy on synthetic-augmented data exceeds accuracy on the original dataset alone
questions.explain: Synthetic data can amplify existing biases if it's generated from biased real data, and may introduce artifacts that don't reflect real-world patterns. Before using synthetic data for a high-stakes credit model, CPMAI requires evaluating whether it preserves statistical fidelity without amplifying bias. Timeline, storage, and accuracy comparisons are secondary to this data validity question.

questions.q: A project manager is presenting AI project results to the board. The model has high accuracy overall but the board asks about performance for minority customer segments. What is the MOST responsible approach?
opts: Present segment-level performance breakdowns and transparently discuss any gaps or disparities found
Present segment-level metrics only for segments specifically mentioned in the original project requirements
Explain that segment-level analysis is planned for the next model iteration and provide overall results now
Present only the overall accuracy since segment-level metrics would require additional statistical analysis
questions.explain: Responsible AI requires transparent disclosure of segment-level performance — especially when asked directly by the governance board. Presenting only aggregate metrics that hide segment disparities is misleading. CPMAI requires complete, honest reporting of AI system performance across population segments, not selective reporting.

questions.q: An AI project team discovers their model's training data was scraped from a website that prohibits data scraping in its terms of service. What should the project manager do IMMEDIATELY?
opts: Anonymize the scraped data to remove any identifiable content before continuing
Request retroactive permission from the website owner before making a final decision
Continue training since the model's outputs do not reproduce the scraped content directly
Stop using the data, consult legal, and assess whether the model must be retrained with properly sourced data
questions.explain: Using data obtained in violation of terms of service is a legal and ethical violation. The immediate action: stop using the data and consult legal — not continue training, anonymize (which doesn't address the legal violation), or wait for retroactive permission while continuing to use the data. Whether the model must be retrained depends on legal assessment of the scope of the violation.

questions.q: A project manager is preparing a model card for stakeholder review before production deployment. Which element is MOST critical to include for a high-impact customer scoring model?
opts: A comparison of the model's performance against five alternative algorithms evaluated during development
The model's hyperparameter configuration and training infrastructure specifications
Known limitations, failure modes, intended use boundaries, and out-of-scope applications
The development team's credentials and experience with similar AI projects
questions.explain: A model card's primary responsible AI purpose is informing stakeholders about known limitations, failure modes, intended use cases, and what the model should NOT be used for. This prevents misuse and informs appropriate governance. Technical specifications, team credentials, and algorithm comparisons serve different audiences and purposes.

questions.q: A project manager receives a request to include age as a training feature in an AI model for health insurance pricing. What should be evaluated FIRST?
opts: Whether age is a strong enough predictor to justify the data pipeline work to include it
Whether using age in insurance pricing is legally permitted in the applicable jurisdiction and consistent with fairness principles
Whether customer age data is available and complete enough across all historical records
Whether age correlates with other existing features that already capture similar predictive signal
questions.explain: Age in insurance pricing is a protected characteristic in many jurisdictions with specific legal constraints. CPMAI requires evaluating legal permissibility and fairness implications before any data feature is included in a consequential model. Predictive strength, data availability, and correlation analysis are secondary to the legal and ethical assessment.

questions.q: Stakeholders ask the project manager whether the AI model's decisions will be reversible if errors are found after deployment. What governance artifact addresses this MOST directly?
opts: The rollback plan and human override procedure in the operationalization plan
The experiment tracking log showing all model versions evaluated during development
The data lineage documentation showing training data sources and transformation steps
The model card documenting known limitations and intended use scope
questions.explain: A rollback plan and human override procedure directly address the question of reversibility and error correction after deployment. These are operationalization governance artifacts specifying how to revert to a prior model version and how humans can override model decisions. The model card, experiment log, and data lineage serve different governance purposes.

questions.q: A stakeholder asks the project manager why the AI model selected a specific customer for a promotional offer. The model is a gradient boosting ensemble. The project manager should FIRST:
opts: Redirect the stakeholder to the data science team for a technical model explanation
Confirm the model meets regulatory requirements, which satisfies the explanation obligation
Explain that gradient boosting models are proprietary and details cannot be shared externally
Provide a feature importance report showing which variables most influenced the specific selection
questions.explain: Stakeholders asking why a specific AI decision was made need an interpretable, business-relevant explanation — feature importance for that specific prediction. Claiming proprietary protection, redirecting to data science, or citing regulatory compliance without answering the question all fail the transparency obligation to affected parties.

questions.q: A project manager is reviewing an AI solution for a children's educational platform. Which responsible AI consideration takes HIGHEST priority for this specific context?
opts: Teacher and parent adoption rates as indicators of educational value delivered
Model accuracy in identifying learning gaps compared to benchmark assessments
Additional safeguards for minor users including strict data minimization and content safety controls
Scalability of the model to support projected user growth over the next five years
questions.explain: AI systems for minor users require additional protections: strict data minimization (collecting only what's necessary for the educational purpose), content safety controls (preventing harmful content exposure), and heightened privacy protections. These take priority over accuracy benchmarks, scalability, and adoption metrics for child-facing AI systems.

sections.title: Domain II: Identify Business Needs & Solutions (Phase I)
sections.description: Problem framing · AI vs non-AI · Feasibility · Success criteria · Stakeholder alignment · ROI

questions.q: During Phase I, the project team identifies that the AI model will require ongoing retraining every quarter to maintain performance. The sponsor was not aware of this ongoing cost. What should the project manager do?
opts: Absorb the retraining cost within the current project budget without informing the sponsor
Include ongoing retraining costs in the total cost of ownership presented to the sponsor before commitment
Defer retraining planning to the Phase V operationalization stage when costs can be estimated more precisely
Reduce the model's scope so retraining is only needed annually to minimize ongoing cost impact
questions.explain: Total cost of ownership for an AI solution includes ongoing retraining, monitoring, and maintenance — not just initial development. Sponsors must understand these ongoing costs before committing to the project. Discovering them post-commitment is a scope and budget failure. CPMAI Phase I requires presenting complete TCO, not just development costs.

questions.q: During Phase I scoping, a business analyst proposes including five additional AI use cases to maximize value from the project investment. The project manager should:
opts: Conduct a separate feasibility assessment for each proposed addition before expanding the scope
Accept the highest-value use case from the five and reject the rest to maintain project focus
Defer the decision until Phase III when the team better understands the technical feasibility of each addition
Accept all five since a broader scope increases the potential return on project investment
questions.explain: Each proposed AI use case requires its own Phase I feasibility assessment — data availability, AI vs. non-AI comparison, ethical considerations, and success criteria definition. Bundling them into a scope expansion without individual assessment risks committing to infeasible or inappropriate use cases. Scope decisions must be based on evidence from assessment, not on optimism about value.

questions.q: What does CPMAI methodology consider the MOST common reason AI projects fail to deliver ROI?
opts: Insufficient compute infrastructure to train models at the required scale
Lack of data science expertise within the internal project team
Selecting an inappropriate machine learning algorithm for the prediction task
Misaligned business problem definition — the AI solves the wrong problem or a problem that doesn't require AI
questions.explain: CPMAI's primary identified cause of AI project failure is misaligned business problem definition — the team builds technically successful AI that solves a problem the business doesn't actually have, or solves a problem that didn't require AI in the first place. Algorithm selection, infrastructure, and team expertise are secondary causes that matter only after the right problem is correctly defined.

questions.q: A project sponsor insists that the AI model must achieve higher accuracy than the existing human process before deployment. The project manager's assessment suggests human baseline measurement will take eight weeks. What should the project manager do?
opts: Propose deploying the model in parallel with the human process to measure the gap in real time
Skip baseline measurement and use industry benchmarks as a proxy for the human baseline
Plan the eight-week baseline measurement as a required Phase I activity before committing to any accuracy targets
Accept a lower accuracy standard to meet the project timeline and rebaseline against human performance post-deployment
questions.explain: If the success criterion is beating human performance, measuring the human baseline is a required Phase I activity. Without knowing the human baseline, there is no meaningful accuracy target and no way to declare success. Eight weeks is a real project cost that must be planned for — not circumvented with benchmarks or deferred to post-deployment measurement.

questions.q: An executive asks the project manager to start an AI project that will 'improve operational efficiency.' What is the MOST important first step?
opts: Assess available infrastructure and compute capacity for the AI workload
Facilitate a problem definition session to identify specific, measurable operational outcomes
Review competitor AI implementations to identify proven efficiency improvement approaches
Develop a preliminary project timeline and resource plan for executive approval
questions.explain: 'Improve operational efficiency' is not a usable AI problem statement. CPMAI Phase I requires translating vague business interest into specific, measurable outcomes — which process, measured by what metric, with what target, over what timeframe. Infrastructure, benchmarking, and planning all depend on this definition first.

questions.q: A project manager is evaluating whether an AI model or a statistical model (e.g., logistic regression) is more appropriate for a churn prediction use case. The business has clean, structured historical data. What factor MOST favors the statistical model?
opts: Statistical models produce interpretable coefficients that business stakeholders can understand and audit directly
Statistical models are less expensive to license than enterprise AI model development platforms
Statistical models require less training data than AI models for the same prediction task
Statistical models are faster to deploy since they require less compute infrastructure than AI models
questions.explain: Interpretable coefficients that stakeholders can understand and audit directly is the primary governance advantage of statistical models over complex AI models for structured prediction tasks. When data is clean and structured, a logistic regression may perform comparably to a complex model while being far more interpretable and auditable. CPMAI requires considering interpretability needs, not just technical performance.

questions.q: The project manager is presenting Phase I findings to the steering committee. The AI use case is feasible but carries moderate ethical risk. What should the presentation include?
opts: A summary indicating ethical risks exist without details to avoid alarming the committee before mitigations are defined
The ethical risk assessment only if the committee specifically requests it during the presentation
Only the technical and business case information since ethical risk assessment is the committee's own responsibility
The ethical risk assessment with specific risks identified, proposed mitigations, and a recommendation for governance decision
questions.explain: The steering committee's role is making informed governance decisions. The project manager must present the ethical risk assessment with specific risks, proposed mitigations, and a clear recommendation — giving the committee everything needed to make an informed go/no-go decision. Withholding risk details, presenting vague summaries, or sharing only on request all deprive the committee of information they need.

questions.q: A project's success criteria state: 'The model should be at least 90% accurate.' The business owner cannot explain what 90% means for actual business outcomes. What should the project manager do?
opts: Proceed with 90% as the technical target and measure business outcomes separately after deployment
Accept 90% accuracy as the success criterion since it is specific and measurable
Work with the business owner to translate accuracy into a business outcome metric before finalizing success criteria
Increase the accuracy target to 95% as a buffer to ensure the model delivers real value
questions.explain: Accuracy is a technical metric, not a business outcome. If the business owner cannot explain what 90% accuracy means for real decisions, the success criterion is not business-aligned. CPMAI Phase I requires translating technical metrics into business outcome measures — what improves, by how much, for what customers. Only then can the project team know what they are actually trying to achieve.

questions.q: A project manager completes a Phase I feasibility study and finds the proposed AI solution would take 18 months to deploy but a process redesign could achieve similar results in 6 months at lower cost. What should the project manager recommend?
opts: Recommend AI since the organization needs to build AI capability regardless of the immediate business case
Present both options to the sponsor with honest cost, timeline, and outcome comparisons and recommend the process redesign
Recommend a hybrid approach that includes both process redesign and AI to maximize organizational learning
Recommend AI to avoid the organizational resistance that process redesign typically generates
questions.explain: CPMAI explicitly requires honest evaluation of AI vs. non-AI alternatives. When a non-AI solution achieves comparable results faster and cheaper, the project manager's obligation is to present this recommendation clearly — not to default to AI for capability-building or resistance-avoidance reasons. The sponsor needs accurate information to make an informed investment decision.

questions.q: An AI project manager is asked to include a customer sentiment analysis component that was not in the original scope. The request comes during Phase II data preparation. What is the MOST appropriate response?
opts: Evaluate the request through a change control process assessing impact on data, timeline, and model complexity
Defer the request to a future project phase since scope changes are not permitted after Phase I
Accept the request only if the sponsor agrees to extend the project timeline by two weeks
Add the component since it uses data already being collected and requires minimal additional effort
questions.explain: Any scope change — including 'minimal effort' additions — requires change control: assessing data requirements, timeline impact, model complexity changes, and alignment with original success criteria. The project manager cannot unilaterally accept, defer, or conditionally accept scope changes without a formal assessment. CPMAI requires structured change management regardless of perceived effort level.

questions.q: A project manager needs to estimate the ROI of a proposed AI churn reduction model. The sponsor asks for a definitive ROI figure before Phase I is complete. What should the project manager provide?
opts: A conservative single estimate to avoid overpromising while Phase I finalizes findings
A deferral explaining that ROI cannot be estimated until after the model is trained and evaluated in Phase III
A precise ROI figure based on industry average churn reduction rates for similar AI models
A range estimate with explicit assumptions and sensitivity analysis, noting that definitive ROI requires Phase I completion
questions.explain: CPMAI requires honest uncertainty communication. Before Phase I completion, ROI estimates are necessarily approximate and assumption-dependent. A range with explicit assumptions and sensitivity analysis is more honest and useful than a false-precision single figure or a deferral to Phase III. The sponsor can make informed decisions from a range — not from overconfident precision or indefinite deferral.

questions.q: A sponsor wants the AI project to achieve measurable results within 90 days of kickoff. The project manager's Phase I assessment indicates the data preparation phase alone will take 60 days. What should the project manager do FIRST?
opts: Identify a pre-built model or off-the-shelf AI tool that can be deployed within 90 days as an alternative
Compress the data preparation timeline to 30 days to accommodate the 90-day expectation
Communicate the realistic timeline based on Phase I findings and facilitate a scope or expectations discussion
Accept the 90-day target and defer data quality work to Phase III to meet the commitment
questions.explain: CPMAI requires project managers to communicate realistic timelines based on evidence — not compress critical phase activities to meet sponsor expectations. Phase I findings are the basis for honest timeline communication. Facilitating a scope or expectations discussion (smaller initial scope, revised milestones, phased delivery) is the correct response to a timeline-feasibility gap.

questions.q: Two competing AI use cases are being evaluated in Phase I. Use Case A has high potential value but requires data not currently available. Use Case B has moderate value and uses existing data. What should the project manager recommend?
opts: Recommend Use Case A since higher value should always take priority in investment decisions
Recommend Use Case B as the first initiative while planning data acquisition for Use Case A in a future phase
Recommend both simultaneously to demonstrate the organization's commitment to AI transformation
Recommend neither until a third use case with both high value and available data is identified
questions.explain: CPMAI prioritization considers both value and feasibility. Use Case B is feasible now; Use Case A is infeasible until data is available. Recommending Use Case B first while building toward Use Case A is sound phased delivery — delivering value with available resources while planning for higher-value future initiatives. Recommending only high-value or only feasible ignores the other critical dimension.

questions.q: A project manager is asked to determine whether AI is needed for a use case that involves routing incoming customer emails to the correct service department. What factor MOST indicates AI is necessary?
opts: Email volume is high and the routing logic is too complex or variable to encode in explicit rules reliably
The current manual routing process takes more than 24 hours to respond to high volumes
The service departments have requested automation to reduce their incoming queue volume
The organization wants to demonstrate its AI capabilities to customers and investors
questions.explain: AI is justified when routing logic is too complex or variable to define in explicit rules. High volume, slow response, and departmental requests are drivers for automation — but they don't determine whether AI (machine learning classification) or a non-AI rules engine is the right tool. The cognitive complexity of the routing decision is the key differentiator.

questions.q: An AI project is in Phase I. The project manager learns a similar AI initiative failed at a competitor due to poor data quality. What should the project manager do with this information?
opts: Present it to the sponsor as evidence the use case is too risky to pursue at this time
Share it with the data science team to inform their algorithm selection decisions
Note it as an external risk and continue Phase I without changing the assessment approach
Use it to specifically strengthen the data quality assessment in Phase II feasibility planning
questions.explain: A known failure pattern at a competitor due to the same risk category (data quality) should directly strengthen that specific assessment area. CPMAI uses lessons learned from industry failures to improve project planning. Noting it without acting misses the learning opportunity. Presenting it as a project-killing risk overstates what is known. Algorithm selection isn't the root issue.

questions.q: A project manager discovers that a proposed AI model will replicate a manually applied decision that legal has already determined to be discriminatory. What should happen IMMEDIATELY?
opts: Consult with the data science team to determine whether the model can be adjusted to reduce discrimination
Redesign the model to avoid replicating the discriminatory decision logic
Halt the project and escalate to legal and the responsible AI governance body before any further development
Document the legal risk in the project risk register and proceed with development while legal reviews
questions.explain: Building an AI model that replicates a known legally discriminatory decision is a critical legal and ethical failure requiring immediate escalation — not documentation or data science consultation. The project must be halted pending legal and governance review. An AI model that automates illegal discrimination is worse than the manual process it replaces because it scales and legitimizes the discrimination.

questions.q: A Phase I assessment concludes that AI is technically feasible but the business outcome is unclear. The sponsor wants to proceed anyway. What is the BEST action?
opts: Proceed since technical feasibility is the primary gate for Phase I approval
Decline to proceed until the business outcome is defined, as unclear outcomes make success impossible to evaluate
Proceed with a pilot to gather data that will clarify the business outcome during development
Present the uncertainty to the steering committee and let them decide whether to proceed without defined outcomes
questions.explain: CPMAI does not allow progression without defined business outcomes. Without knowing what success looks like, there is no basis for go/no-go decisions, no way to evaluate whether the AI is working, and no accountability for the investment. Pilots can explore technical approaches, but the business outcome must be defined before the pilot — not discovered during it.

questions.q: During Phase I, a project manager determines that the AI project will require personal customer data but the organization's privacy policy prohibits using that data category for this purpose. What is the correct action?
opts: Update the privacy policy before starting the project to permit the data use
Redesign the solution to achieve the business outcome without requiring the prohibited data category
Obtain individual customer consent to override the organizational privacy policy restriction
Use the data anyway since the business case justifies a policy exception for this project
questions.explain: If the proposed solution requires data prohibited by the organization's privacy policy, the solution must be redesigned to work without that data. Unilaterally using prohibited data, updating policies solely to permit a specific project, or seeking individual consent to override an organizational policy are all improper. The solution must fit within existing data governance constraints or the project scope must be adjusted.

questions.q: A business unit wants AI to predict which employees are likely to be promoted in the next 12 months. During Phase I, the project manager identifies this as ethically problematic. What is the MOST likely concern?
opts: The historical promotion data may be insufficient in volume for reliable model training
Employees may resist the initiative, reducing adoption and limiting the solution's practical value
The model may not achieve sufficient accuracy given the subjectivity involved in promotion decisions
Predicting promotions creates privacy, fairness, and potential self-fulfilling prophecy risks that may harm individuals and undermine organizational fairness
questions.explain: Predicting which employees will be promoted creates multiple ethical problems: privacy (using employee data to predict personal career outcomes), fairness (if biased data determines who the AI predicts will advance, it creates self-fulfilling discrimination), and the risk that predictions become decisions that replace genuine performance assessment. These are the substantive Phase I ethical concerns, not accuracy, data volume, or resistance.

questions.q: A project team proposes using the organization's AI project as a vehicle to build general AI capabilities for future use. The current business case supports only the specific use case. What should the project manager do?
opts: Defer capability building to a post-project retrospective when lessons can be systematically captured
Support the broader capability-building goal since AI capability is a strategic organizational asset
Scope the project to deliver the defined use case and propose a separate capability-building initiative if one is desired
Expand the project scope to include capability building and seek additional budget from the sponsor
questions.explain: Project scope must match the approved business case. Building general AI capabilities beyond the defined use case is scope creep requiring separate business case and approval. The project manager should deliver what was approved and propose a separate initiative for capability building — not expand scope without authorization, even for strategically valuable reasons.

questions.q: During Phase I, the project team discovers the proposed AI use case could be achieved with a vendor's pre-built solution. The sponsor prefers building internally. What should the project manager present?
opts: Proceed with internal build since organizational preference for internal development is a valid project constraint
Present an objective build-vs-buy analysis including cost, timeline, customization needs, and vendor risk to inform the decision
Select the vendor solution since build-vs-buy decisions should always favor faster time to value
Defer the decision and prototype both options simultaneously to gather comparative evidence
questions.explain: Build-vs-buy is a Phase I decision requiring objective analysis — not sponsor preference or a general rule favoring one approach. The project manager presents an honest comparison of cost, timeline, customization requirements, and vendor risk. Sponsor preference is an input, not the decision criterion. The decision should be made on evidence.

sections.title: Domain III: Identify Data Needs (Phase II)
sections.description: Data quality · Governance · Lineage · Feature engineering · DataOps · Bias in data

questions.q: During Phase II, the team discovers that the positive class (churn = yes) represents only 2% of the training data. What is the MOST important action to include in the data preparation plan?
opts: Expand the training dataset by collecting additional records until the positive class reaches at least 10%
Address the class imbalance through resampling techniques, cost-sensitive learning, or adjusted evaluation metrics before model training
Proceed with the imbalanced dataset and rely on the model to identify the minority class patterns naturally
Select a model architecture specifically designed to handle extreme class imbalance without preprocessing
questions.explain: 2% class prevalence is severe imbalance — standard training will produce a model that predicts 'no churn' for nearly everyone, appearing highly accurate while missing all churners. CPMAI Phase II data preparation must include an explicit class imbalance strategy: oversampling (SMOTE), undersampling, cost-sensitive learning, or at minimum adjusting evaluation metrics from accuracy to precision-recall. This is a Phase II plan item, not a Phase III discovery.

questions.q: An AI model for fraud detection requires near-real-time transaction data during deployment. During Phase II, the team discovers the data pipeline delivers data with a 4-hour delay. What must be assessed?
opts: Whether the 4-hour delay makes the model unfit for real-time fraud detection and whether the pipeline can be improved or the use case redesigned
Whether the fraud detection threshold can be adjusted to maintain acceptable false positive rates with delayed data
Whether the 4-hour delay affects Phase II training data preparation timelines
Whether the model can be retrained more frequently to compensate for the 4-hour data delay
questions.explain: A 4-hour data delay for near-real-time fraud detection is a fundamental fitness-for-purpose problem — by the time the model scores a transaction, 4 hours have passed. This is a go/no-go issue requiring assessment of whether the pipeline can be improved to meet latency requirements or whether the use case needs to be redesigned. Threshold adjustment and retraining don't solve a latency problem.

questions.q: A project team has collected training data but it contains duplicate records due to a system migration. What is the MOST important reason to remove duplicates before model training?
opts: Duplicate records create version control issues in the data pipeline and experiment tracking logs
Duplicate records cause confusion in the labeling workflow when annotators encounter identical cases
Duplicate records increase storage costs and slow down model training unnecessarily
Duplicate records inflate the representation of certain patterns and can cause the model to overfit to repeated examples
questions.explain: Duplicate records inflate the weight of specific patterns in training — the model sees those examples more often and learns to weight them disproportionately, leading to overfitting. This is a model quality concern. Storage cost and training speed are efficiency concerns. Version control and labeling confusion are process concerns. Model quality is the primary issue.

questions.q: An AI project team is about to begin Phase II data preparation when a new regulation takes effect requiring all customer data used in AI systems to be anonymized. What is the MOST appropriate action?
opts: Complete Phase II as planned and apply anonymization during Phase V before the model touches production data
Proceed with the original data plan and submit a regulatory exception request immediately
Pause data preparation, assess the regulation's applicability, and revise the data plan to comply before proceeding
Anonymize only the test and validation sets since training data is internal and not subject to the regulation
questions.explain: A new regulation affecting data use requires immediate assessment and compliance before proceeding. Pausing to assess applicability and revising the data plan is the correct CPMAI response — not proceeding and seeking exceptions, not partial anonymization, and not deferring compliance to Phase V. Regulations take effect regardless of project stage.

questions.q: A data scientist uses a log transformation on a financial transaction amount feature to normalize its distribution. The project manager should ensure this transformation is:
opts: Applied only during training to avoid the computational overhead of real-time transformation during scoring
Documented and applied consistently in both training and the production scoring pipeline to prevent training-serving skew
Validated by the business team since log transformations change the interpretability of financial features
Reviewed by the auditor before implementation since transformations affect regulatory model documentation requirements
questions.explain: Any feature transformation applied during training must be applied identically during production scoring — inconsistent transformations between training and serving create training-serving skew, causing the model to receive different feature distributions than it was trained on. Documentation and pipeline consistency are the core requirements. Business validation and audit review are secondary.

questions.q: A project team is building an AI model to predict loan default. During data preparation, a data engineer flags that the dataset was collected during an economic recession and may not represent normal economic conditions. What risk should the project manager document?
opts: Distribution shift risk — model trained on recession data may underperform when economic conditions normalize
Feature engineering risk — recession-period feature correlations may differ from standard feature relationships
Model complexity risk — recession data requires more complex algorithms to capture the unusual patterns
Sample size risk — recession periods may have generated fewer loan records than typical periods
questions.explain: Training on recession-period data creates distribution shift risk — the model learns patterns specific to economic stress conditions that may not predict default accurately in normal economic conditions. This must be documented as a key risk affecting model reliability. The other risks (sample size, feature engineering, complexity) are secondary to the fundamental representativeness problem.

questions.q: A project team in Phase II discovers that one feature they planned to use is only available in production 48 hours after a customer interaction, but the model needs to score in real time. What should be done?
opts: Retrain the model using only features available in real time and treat the 48-hour feature as a post-hoc validation input
Flag the issue in the risk register and proceed — the production team will resolve the data pipeline during deployment
Use the feature in training and accept the 48-hour delay in production scoring for that feature type
Remove the feature from the model or find a proxy feature available in real time
questions.explain: A feature unavailable at scoring time causes training-serving skew — the model is trained with information it will never have during real-time prediction. The feature must be removed from the model or replaced with a real-time proxy. Accepting the 48-hour delay, treating it as post-hoc input, or deferring to production all introduce the same fundamental training-serving gap.

questions.q: A project manager is reviewing a data inventory document for Phase II. The document lists data sources but does not include legal consent basis for each source. What action is required?
opts: Verify consent basis only for data sources flagged by the legal team during their review
Accept the inventory as sufficient since data consent is a legal team responsibility, not a project management concern
Require the data inventory to be updated with the legal basis for using each data source before Phase II proceeds
Add consent basis documentation as a Phase III activity when the model moves toward deployment
questions.explain: CPMAI Phase II data inventory must include the legal basis for using each data source — this is a governance requirement, not a legal team responsibility to be delegated. Using data without a documented legal basis creates compliance risk that invalidates the entire model. The project manager must require this information as a Phase II gate criterion.

questions.q: A project manager learns that the data engineering team deleted the raw training data after creating processed features to save storage costs. What problem does this create?
opts: The model cannot be validated since raw data is required for the test set evaluation
The model cannot be reproduced from scratch if the feature engineering logic is found to be flawed
Regulatory auditors will require the raw data for compliance review and its deletion creates a compliance gap
Future team members cannot understand how features were derived without the raw data for reference
questions.explain: Deleting raw data after feature engineering removes the ability to reproduce the model from scratch — if the feature engineering logic is later found to be incorrect or biased, there's no way to reprocess the raw data. This violates reproducibility requirements. CPMAI requires raw data to be retained (with appropriate access controls) for the model's operational lifetime.

questions.q: A data scientist suggests storing raw training data, processed features, and model artifacts together in a single repository for simplicity. What concern should the project manager raise?
opts: Combining raw data, features, and model artifacts makes it difficult to audit, version, and reproduce each component independently
A single repository requires a centralized access control system that increases IT infrastructure complexity
Storing models with data creates a security risk since data scientists can access model artifacts directly
A single repository creates storage inefficiency since raw data is much larger than processed features
questions.explain: Raw data, processed features, and model artifacts serve different governance and operational purposes and should be separately versioned, tracked, and controlled. Combining them makes it difficult to reproduce a specific model version from its source data, audit changes to features independently, or apply different access controls to sensitive raw data vs. processed outputs.

questions.q: A project manager reviews a Phase II data plan that does not include a data validation step before model training begins. What risk does this omission create?
opts: The team may miss opportunities to add additional features identified during the validation review process
Regulators may require retrospective validation that disrupts the project timeline after development is underway
The model may be trained on corrupted, inconsistent, or out-of-scope data without any detection mechanism in place
The absence of validation increases Phase III model development costs due to additional debugging requirements
questions.explain: Without data validation before training, errors, inconsistencies, or out-of-scope records enter the training set undetected — potentially producing a model that appears to train successfully but fails in production because it learned from bad data. Detecting data problems after the model is built is far more expensive than catching them before training. Data validation is a required Phase II gate.

questions.q: The data science team wants to use all available historical data going back 15 years to maximize training set size. The project manager should challenge this because:
opts: Older data requires additional preprocessing that will delay Phase II completion beyond the planned timeline
15 years of data exceeds typical infrastructure capacity for efficient model training runs
Data from 15 years ago may reflect obsolete business rules, customer segments, or market conditions irrelevant to current predictions
Using more than 5 years of historical data violates PMI-CPMAI methodology guidelines on training data scope
