questions.explain: Historical data older than a certain threshold may reflect conditions that no longer exist — discontinued products, obsolete business rules, different customer demographics, pre-regulatory environments. Including irrelevant historical patterns in training may degrade model performance on current predictions. Data relevance is as important as data volume.

questions.q: A data engineering team reports that 30% of records in the training dataset have null values for a key predictive feature. What should the project manager ensure is evaluated BEFORE any imputation strategy is applied?
opts: Whether the imputation algorithm will increase model training time beyond acceptable limits
Whether the missing data is random or follows a systematic pattern related to the target variable or demographic groups
Whether removing the 30% of affected records would leave sufficient data for model training
Whether a data vendor can supply the missing values at a cost within the Phase II budget
questions.explain: Understanding why data is missing — random (MCAR), related to other variables (MAR), or related to the missing variable itself (MNAR) — is essential before choosing an imputation strategy. Systematic missingness correlated with demographic groups or the target variable introduces bias regardless of the imputation method chosen. The nature of missingness determines the appropriate treatment.

questions.q: During Phase II, the project manager learns the training data was originally collected for a marketing optimization purpose, not for the current AI use case. What must be evaluated?
opts: Whether using data collected for marketing for a different AI purpose complies with the original consent given
Whether the data quantity collected for marketing is sufficient for the current model's training requirements
Whether the data engineering team can restructure the marketing dataset for the new use case efficiently
Whether the data schema is compatible with the current model's feature requirements
questions.explain: Purpose limitation is a core data privacy principle — using data collected for one purpose (marketing) for a different purpose (a new AI model) may violate the consent under which it was collected. This must be evaluated before the data is used. Schema compatibility, data quantity, and restructuring efficiency are secondary technical concerns that only matter if purpose compliance is established.

questions.q: An AI project team discovers that the best available training dataset comes from a different country than where the model will be deployed. What is the MOST critical concern to evaluate?
opts: Whether the foreign dataset is labeled in a language that the model development team can process
Whether accessing the foreign dataset requires international data licensing agreements
Whether data transfer across international boundaries complies with data protection regulations
Whether the population, behaviors, and patterns in the foreign dataset are representative of the target deployment population
questions.explain: The representativeness of foreign data for the target deployment population is the most critical concern — a model trained on patterns from one country's population may fail to predict reliably for a different country's population with different demographics, behaviors, or economic conditions. Data transfer compliance and licensing are important but secondary to whether the data is even appropriate for the use case.

questions.q: The project team has collected training data but the data science lead wants to begin feature engineering before the data governance review is complete. What should the project manager decide?
opts: Grant an exception for high-priority features while continuing the governance review for remaining data sources
Allow feature engineering to proceed since the governance review focuses on access controls, not feature design
Require the governance review to complete before feature engineering begins, since governance findings may change which data is usable
Allow feature engineering to proceed in parallel but flag results as provisional until governance is confirmed
questions.explain: Governance review may determine that certain data sources cannot be used due to consent, legal, or quality issues. Building features on data that subsequently fails governance review wastes effort and may require complete rework. CPMAI requires governance gates to complete before dependent work proceeds — not in parallel, and not with exceptions for priority features.

questions.q: A team is preparing labeled training data for a text classification model. The labeling guidelines are ambiguous, and different annotators interpret edge cases differently. What should the project manager require BEFORE full-scale annotation begins?
opts: Begin annotation immediately and use inter-annotator agreement statistics to identify and remove inconsistent labels after the full set is completed
Resolve the ambiguities in labeling guidelines and validate inter-annotator agreement on a sample before full annotation
Assign edge cases to the most experienced annotator to ensure consistent quality across the most difficult examples
Proceed and train multiple models on differently labeled subsets to identify which labeling scheme produces best accuracy
questions.explain: Ambiguous labeling guidelines produce inconsistent training data that reduces model quality and introduces noise. Resolving ambiguities and validating inter-annotator agreement on a representative sample before scaling is far more efficient than annotating at scale and discovering quality problems afterward. CPMAI requires data quality standards to be defined and validated before, not after, large-scale execution.

questions.q: A team building a demand forecasting model wants to include weather data as a feature. The weather data comes from a public API with 99.2% uptime. What concern should the project manager flag?
opts: Public API data lacks the proprietary nature needed for competitive advantage in the demand forecasting model
Weather data may violate data residency requirements if the API provider is in a different jurisdiction
The public API has a usage limit that may be exceeded during high-volume demand forecasting runs
The 0.8% downtime means weather data will occasionally be unavailable, potentially breaking model scoring during outages
questions.explain: A feature from an external API with less than 100% uptime creates a production reliability risk — when the API is unavailable, the model either cannot score or must handle the missing feature gracefully. This must be designed for: implementing fallback strategies or ensuring the model can score without the weather feature when the API is down.

questions.q: A data scientist proposes using a feature derived from customers' ZIP codes to improve a credit model's accuracy. The project manager should flag this PRIMARILY because:
opts: ZIP code data may not be consistently available across all customer records in the training dataset
ZIP code features require additional geocoding infrastructure that increases Phase II development costs
ZIP code derivations typically add model complexity without proportional accuracy improvement
ZIP codes can serve as proxies for race and ethnicity, potentially introducing discriminatory patterns into the credit model
questions.explain: ZIP codes in the US correlate strongly with racial and ethnic demographics due to historical housing segregation. Using them in credit models creates proxy discrimination — the model effectively uses race/ethnicity in its predictions without the feature appearing explicitly. CPMAI requires flagging this proxy bias risk before adding such features to consequential models.

questions.q: An AI project uses customer transaction data from five different regional systems with inconsistent date formats and currency units. What should the project manager prioritize in the Phase II data preparation plan?
opts: Proceeding to feature engineering and resolving format inconsistencies during model development
Selecting the regional system with the highest data quality as the primary training source
Establishing data standardization and normalization procedures before any feature engineering begins
Training a separate model for each regional system to avoid cross-system data quality issues
questions.explain: Inconsistent formats and units across source systems must be standardized before feature engineering — features built on inconsistent data will be unreliable. CPMAI Phase II requires data standardization as a prerequisite to feature engineering, not something to fix during model development. Building on unstandardized data creates compounding quality problems in downstream phases.

sections.title: Domain IV: Manage AI Model Development & Evaluation
sections.description: Model selection · Evaluation metrics · Iteration · Go/no-go · Bias testing · MLOps

questions.q: The project manager is reviewing a proposed model evaluation plan. It includes accuracy, precision, and recall but does not include any bias or fairness metrics. What should the project manager require?
opts: Approve the plan since accuracy, precision, and recall are sufficient for a comprehensive model evaluation
Add fairness and bias metrics evaluated across relevant demographic and population subgroups before approving the plan
Defer the bias assessment to Phase V when production data is available for more representative analysis
Add a narrative statement confirming the team followed responsible AI principles during development as a proxy for quantitative bias metrics
questions.explain: Fairness and bias metrics are required evaluation components — not optional additions, not post-deployment assessments, and not replaceable by narrative statements. A model evaluation plan without bias metrics across population subgroups is incomplete for any use case involving human subjects. CPMAI requires bias evaluation as a standard model evaluation component, not a supplementary review.

questions.q: After model development, the business owner says: 'The model looks good but I'm not sure I trust it.' What should the project manager do NEXT?
opts: Investigate the source of distrust — whether it's unexplainability, unfamiliar prediction patterns, or lack of exposure to model outputs
Ask the data science team to run additional experiments to demonstrate model robustness
Present the model's test set accuracy alongside competitor benchmarks to establish credibility
Reassure the business owner that the model passed all technical evaluation criteria
questions.explain: Business owner distrust is a signal requiring investigation — it may indicate an explainability problem, a pattern in model predictions that conflicts with domain knowledge, or simply insufficient exposure to how the model works. CPMAI requires understanding the root cause of distrust before addressing it. Reassurance, additional experiments, and benchmarks don't diagnose why trust is missing.

questions.q: During model evaluation, the team finds the model performs well on validation data but generates predictions that experienced domain experts say are 'implausible.' What should the project manager investigate FIRST?
opts: Whether the validation set was too small to produce statistically reliable performance estimates
Whether the experts should be excluded from evaluation since they may introduce subjective bias
Whether the domain experts' criteria align with the model's defined success metrics
Whether data leakage is inflating validation performance while masking real-world prediction quality
questions.explain: When domain experts find predictions implausible despite good validation metrics, data leakage is a primary suspect — a feature encoding future information makes the model appear accurate on held-out data while generating predictions the real world cannot support. Domain expert judgment is a valuable signal, not a bias to exclude. Metric alignment and set size are secondary.

questions.q: A project manager reviews experiment tracking records and notices the data scientist ran 47 experiments but only documented the last five. What risk should be raised?
opts: Regulators may require a full experiment log that the team cannot produce without the missing documentation
The team wasted significant compute resources on 42 undocumented experiments that add no value
The selection of the final model cannot be fully audited or reproduced without documentation of the full experiment history
The undocumented experiments may have generated models that performed better than the documented ones
questions.explain: Incomplete experiment documentation creates an audit and reproducibility gap — the final model selection cannot be fully justified or reproduced without knowing what was tried and why it was rejected. Selective documentation also creates questions about whether the selected model is truly the best approach or the one that happened to be documented. Full experiment tracking is a CPMAI professional standard.

questions.q: A model go/no-go review is scheduled. The model meets accuracy and speed requirements but the compliance officer has not yet completed their review. What should the project manager do?
opts: Proceed with the go/no-go decision based on the available technical evidence and add compliance as a post-launch condition
Postpone the go/no-go decision until the compliance review is complete since compliance is a required gate
Record the compliance review as a risk item and proceed to deployment planning while awaiting the outcome
Ask the data science team to provide a compliance summary as a proxy for the compliance officer's review
questions.explain: Compliance review is a required gate — not a post-launch condition, a risk item to manage in parallel, or something that can be proxied by a technical team. Proceeding without compliance sign-off exposes the organization to regulatory risk. The project manager must postpone the go/no-go until all required reviewers have completed their assessments.

questions.q: A project team is preparing for the final model go/no-go review and wants to present the model with the highest accuracy from 80 experiments. The project manager should ensure the final model is selected based on:
opts: Performance on the held-out test set that was not used during any development or selection decisions
A combination of accuracy, inference speed, and model interpretability weighted by project priorities
The steering committee's preference after reviewing a summary of the top five performing model candidates
Highest accuracy on the training set since this shows the model has learned the most from available data
questions.explain: The final model must be selected based on performance on the held-out test set — data the model has never seen during training, validation, or algorithm selection. If 80 experiments were run on the same data, the 'best' model may have simply overfit to that specific dataset. The test set provides the only unbiased estimate of how the model will perform on new, unseen production data.

questions.q: A data scientist proposes evaluating the model using only the metrics specified in the original project requirements, which were defined in Phase I before the model type was selected. The project manager should:
opts: Add all standard ML evaluation metrics regardless of relevance to ensure comprehensive evaluation documentation
Defer any metric adjustments to the sponsor's approval since changing evaluation criteria requires scope change authorization
Agree since using only the original metrics ensures consistency with the approved business case
Review whether the original metrics are appropriate for the model type selected and add supplementary metrics if the original ones are insufficient
questions.explain: Evaluation metrics defined in Phase I may have been based on initial assumptions about the model type. If a different model type was selected, the original metrics may be insufficient or misaligned. CPMAI requires reviewing whether the original metrics remain appropriate and adding supplementary metrics where needed — not blindly using original metrics that may not fit the actual model.

questions.q: A development team wants to use the same dataset for both model selection (choosing between algorithms) and final performance evaluation. The project manager should require:
opts: Documentation confirming the evaluation methodology follows industry standard cross-validation protocols
An expanded total dataset size to ensure the evaluation set is large enough for statistical validity
A separate held-out test set that is not used during algorithm selection or hyperparameter tuning
Agreement from the steering committee since this is a governance decision, not a technical one
questions.explain: Using the same data for algorithm selection and final evaluation creates optimistic bias — the selected algorithm may be the one that happened to fit that specific dataset best, not the one that generalizes best to unseen data. A separate held-out test set not used during any development decisions is the standard solution. This is a technical requirement with governance implications, not a governance decision requiring committee approval.

questions.q: A model developed for predicting customer support call volume achieves excellent performance during weekdays but fails on weekends. The project manager is told weekends represent only 12% of call volume. What should be done?
opts: Accept the model since weekdays represent the majority of business impact and 12% is within acceptable tolerance
Investigate whether weekend call patterns require a separate model or additional training data to achieve acceptable performance across all days
Deploy the model for weekday scheduling only and continue manual forecasting for weekends
Add weekend performance improvement to the next retraining cycle's backlog as a low-priority enhancement
questions.explain: Systematic failure on weekends — even at 12% of volume — may represent a significant operational problem and is a reliability failure for a deployment intended to forecast all days. CPMAI requires investigating whether weekend patterns are fundamentally different and need separate treatment, not accepting failures because they occur on a smaller subset. The 12% volume share doesn't make the failure acceptable.

questions.q: A project team celebrates after achieving 96% accuracy on the test set. The project manager asks: 'What is the baseline?' and the team doesn't know. What concern does this reveal?
opts: Without a baseline — what the existing process or a simple rule achieves — 96% accuracy has no meaningful business context
The team should have agreed on a baseline at the start of Phase III before beginning model development
The 96% accuracy result cannot be trusted without a baseline to confirm the model is not simply predicting the majority class
The team failed to document their experiment tracking log with baseline comparison results
questions.explain: 96% accuracy means nothing without context — if the majority class represents 97% of cases, predicting the majority class always achieves 97% accuracy without any learning. Without a baseline (existing process performance or a simple benchmark), the 96% accuracy cannot be evaluated for business value. CPMAI requires baseline measurement as a foundational element of success evaluation.

questions.q: The model evaluation gate review is complete and results meet all defined criteria. The project manager is about to approve moving to Phase V when a team member mentions they resampled the training data after splitting off the test set. What issue should this trigger?
opts: A data governance review to confirm the resampling approach complies with data policy requirements
A model audit to confirm the resampling did not change the model's predicted probability distributions
A timeline delay since the resampling step needs to be added to the Phase III documentation retroactively
An investigation into whether test data contaminated the resampling process, which would invalidate the evaluation results
questions.explain: Resampling (SMOTE or similar) must be applied only to the training set — not after splitting if there's any risk of test data contamination. If test records influenced the resampled training set, the evaluation is invalid: the model has essentially 'seen' test data during training. This triggers an investigation before Phase V approval, not documentation, governance review, or a distribution audit.

questions.q: A project manager is reviewing evaluation metrics for a model designed to identify patients who need urgent intervention. The data scientist reports 91% precision and 44% recall. What concern should be raised?
opts: The 91%/44% imbalance suggests the validation set may not be representative of the production population
The precision of 91% is too high, suggesting the model is overfitting to the validation set
The recall of 44% means the model misses 56% of patients who need urgent intervention — a patient safety failure
The precision-recall trade-off indicates the classification threshold is set incorrectly for the use case
questions.explain: 44% recall in a medical intervention prioritization model means 56% of patients requiring urgent care are not identified — a patient safety failure. For medical triage, recall (catching all cases needing intervention) must be the priority metric, even at the cost of lower precision (more false alarms). A 44% recall is not a threshold tuning issue; it's a model performance failure for this use case.

questions.q: A model evaluation reveals strong performance on majority class examples but poor performance on a minority class representing a vulnerable customer group. The sponsor says the aggregate metrics meet the threshold. What should the project manager do?
opts: Agree to proceed since aggregate metrics meeting the threshold satisfies the defined success criteria
Flag the minority class performance as an equity and responsible AI concern requiring remediation before deployment
Add a supplementary manual review process for the minority class to compensate for lower model performance
Request the data science team investigate whether a class weighting adjustment could improve minority performance
questions.explain: Aggregate metrics masking poor performance for a vulnerable group is an equity and responsible AI failure — not a success criterion compliance matter. CPMAI requires raising this as a concern requiring remediation before deployment, regardless of whether aggregate thresholds are met. Manual review as a compensating control addresses the symptom, not the root cause. Flagging for remediation is the required first action.

questions.q: A model development team wants to productionize the model themselves to save time rather than going through the MLOps team. The project manager should:
opts: Require the MLOps team to manage productionization to ensure deployment standards, monitoring, and governance controls are applied
Allow a one-time exception for this project and require MLOps involvement for all subsequent AI deployments
Evaluate the development team's deployment capability and decide based on their demonstrated technical competency
Support this approach since development team deployment eliminates the handoff risk and communication overhead
questions.explain: MLOps teams manage productionization to ensure deployment standards, reproducibility, monitoring infrastructure, security controls, and governance are applied consistently — not just to add process overhead. Development teams productionizing their own models frequently skip governance controls, create non-standard deployments, and establish monitoring gaps. CPMAI requires proper MLOps oversight for production deployments.

questions.q: A model achieves 88% accuracy on the test set but the data scientist believes a different algorithm could reach 93%. The project manager must decide whether to continue iterating. What is the MOST important consideration?
opts: Whether the team has sufficient compute capacity to train additional model iterations efficiently
Whether the alternative algorithm introduces new dependencies that complicate the deployment architecture
Whether 93% is achievable within the remaining timeline and budget constraints
Whether 88% already meets the defined business success criterion or whether the additional 5% changes business outcomes materially
questions.explain: The question is whether 88% meets the defined business success criteria — not whether higher accuracy is technically achievable. If 88% already satisfies the business outcome target, additional iteration consumes resources without delivering proportional value. CPMAI requires evaluating model performance against business criteria, not maximizing technical metrics for their own sake.

questions.q: During Phase III model development, the data science team proposes a major architecture change after 10 sprints of development. The change would require starting over. What should the project manager do?
opts: Defer the change to a Phase III extension and deliver the current architecture first for quick value realization
Reject the change since major architecture changes are not permitted after development has begun
Evaluate the justification for the change and compare the cost of pivoting versus the cost of continuing with the current approach
Accept the change immediately since the data science team has the technical expertise to assess when a pivot is needed
questions.explain: Major architecture changes in mid-development require rigorous justification — is the evidence compelling enough that continuing on the current path is worse than the cost of starting over? The project manager evaluates this trade-off with the data science team and stakeholders, not unilaterally rejecting or accepting. The answer depends on the quality of the evidence and the cost differential.

questions.q: A classification model achieves high F1 score on the test set but performs poorly when the operations team tests it with real production inputs. What is the MOST likely explanation?
opts: The operations team is testing incorrectly and their inputs do not match the model's expected feature schema
The test set used for evaluation was not representative of actual production input distributions
The model's hyperparameters were tuned for test set performance and need adjustment for production inputs
The production environment is processing inputs differently than the development evaluation environment
questions.explain: A gap between test set performance and production performance most commonly indicates that the test set was not representative of actual production inputs — it may have been from a different time period, customer segment, or data collection process. This is a data representativeness failure in Phase II that surfaces during testing.

questions.q: The project team is choosing between a neural network (higher accuracy) and a decision tree (more interpretable) for a model that will support parole recommendations. Which should the project manager recommend?
opts: The decision tree since interpretability is essential for decisions affecting individual liberty that must be explainable and auditable
The neural network with SHAP explanations added post-hoc to satisfy interpretability requirements
Present both options to the governance board without a recommendation since the choice involves value trade-offs
The neural network since higher accuracy reduces misclassification errors in consequential decisions
questions.explain: Parole recommendations affect individual liberty and must be explainable to affected individuals, legal representatives, and oversight bodies. A decision tree's native interpretability is more defensible than post-hoc SHAP explanations for a neural network in a legally contested context. CPMAI requires matching model selection to governance and accountability requirements, not just optimizing accuracy.

questions.q: A model was evaluated six months ago and approved for deployment. Before launch, the project manager should verify:
opts: That the model documentation was updated to reflect any organizational changes during the delay
That the compute infrastructure provisioned six months ago still meets current scaling requirements
That the development team is still available to support the model post-deployment
That the evaluation results remain valid given any data distribution changes in the six-month gap
questions.explain: A six-month gap between evaluation and deployment means real-world data may have changed — the model approved on one data distribution may underperform on the current distribution. The project manager must verify that the evaluation results still represent expected production performance before proceeding to launch. Documentation and infrastructure are secondary validity concerns.

sections.title: Domain V: Operationalize AI Solution (Phase V)
sections.description: Deployment readiness · SLOs · Monitoring · Drift · Incident response · Rollback · Decommission

questions.q: Six months after deployment, the model's business sponsor is replaced by a new executive who is unfamiliar with the AI system. What operational action should the project manager take?
opts: Update the project documentation to reflect the new sponsor's contact information
Brief the new executive on the model's purpose, performance metrics, known limitations, and governance responsibilities
Transfer full operational responsibility to the MLOps team since executive sponsorship transitions are out of scope
Notify the governance board that sponsor succession creates a governance gap requiring a temporary review committee
questions.explain: Executive sponsor transitions for deployed AI systems require active briefing on model purpose, performance, known limitations, and governance accountabilities — the new executive needs to understand what they're now responsible for overseeing. Updating documentation, transferring to MLOps, or escalating to a committee are all secondary to ensuring the new sponsor can exercise informed oversight.

questions.q: A pilot is complete and the model has met all functional acceptance criteria. Before approving production deployment, what should the project manager verify FIRST?
opts: That SLOs, monitoring infrastructure, incident response procedures, and rollback capability are all in place
That the business team has communicated the deployment to end users and scheduled adoption training
That the compute infrastructure provisioned for the pilot can scale to full production volume
That the development team has completed all planned model documentation for the project archive
questions.explain: Meeting functional acceptance criteria in a pilot is necessary but not sufficient for production approval. CPMAI requires verifying operational readiness: SLOs defined, monitoring infrastructure active, incident response procedures documented, and rollback capability confirmed. Without these, a technically passing pilot can fail catastrophically in production without anyone able to detect or recover quickly.

questions.q: A model has been in production for 12 months and performance metrics remain above the defined thresholds. No retraining trigger has fired. Should the project manager schedule a proactive review?
opts: No — proactive reviews should only occur when stakeholders request them or external events create obvious risk
Yes — models should be retrained on an annual schedule regardless of performance metrics to maintain data freshness
No — the monitoring system is working correctly and a proactive review is unnecessary overhead
Yes — even without triggered thresholds, annual review should assess whether business context or requirements have changed enough to warrant model updates
questions.explain: Monitoring thresholds detect measurable degradation but may not detect changes in business context, regulatory requirements, or strategic priorities that haven't yet degraded model metrics. An annual proactive review assesses whether the model still addresses the right business problem under current conditions — separate from performance threshold monitoring. Fixed annual retraining without performance assessment wastes resources.

questions.q: A project manager is planning an AI solution decommission. The model has been in production for three years and several downstream systems depend on it. What is the FIRST step in the decommissioning plan?
opts: Conduct a final performance review to document the model's historical accuracy for organizational records
Schedule the shutdown date and notify all stakeholders of the decommissioning timeline
Map all downstream system dependencies and confirm each has a transition plan before setting the shutdown date
Archive the model artifacts and documentation before initiating any operational changes
questions.explain: Decommissioning must begin with dependency mapping — identifying all downstream systems that consume the model's outputs and confirming each has a transition plan. Shutting down a model before all dependencies are migrated causes operational failures across dependent systems. Notifications, archival, and performance reviews are all important but cannot precede dependency confirmation.

questions.q: The operations team asks the project manager what the 'acceptable error rate' is for the deployed AI model. The project manager realizes this was never defined. What should be done?
opts: Establish an informal acceptable error rate based on the model's test set performance and communicate it to operations
Work with the business and governance team to formally define error rate SLOs before production operations depend on an undefined standard
Use industry benchmark error rates for similar AI systems as the operational standard until internal standards are defined
Defer the definition to the data science team who best understand the model's error characteristics
questions.explain: SLOs (Service Level Objectives) including acceptable error rates must be formally defined with the business and governance team before they're needed for operations decisions. An informal PM estimate, an industry benchmark, or a data science definition all substitute a governance decision with a technical one. CPMAI requires formal business-aligned SLO definition as a Phase V prerequisite to production operations.

questions.q: An AI model is being retrained on new data after detected drift. Before the retrained model replaces the current production model, what must happen?
opts: The retrained model must pass the same evaluation criteria as the original before it replaces the production model
The data science team must document the specific drift patterns detected before the retrained model is promoted
The compliance team must review the new training data to confirm no new data governance issues were introduced
The project sponsor must formally approve the retraining since it constitutes a model update
questions.explain: A retrained model is a new model version — it must pass the same evaluation gates (accuracy, bias testing, fairness metrics, business success criteria) as the original before replacing the production model. Retraining to address drift and then deploying without re-evaluation risks trading one performance problem for another. Sponsor approval, documentation, and compliance review are supplementary steps.

questions.q: The model monitoring dashboard shows prediction volume dropped by 60% unexpectedly last Tuesday. Performance metrics for predictions made appear normal. What should the project manager investigate FIRST?
opts: Whether users manually bypassed the AI model for 60% of decisions due to a loss of confidence
Whether the monitoring system itself is experiencing data collection issues that underreport actual prediction volume
Whether a data drift event occurred last Tuesday that caused the model to reject 60% of incoming inputs
Whether an upstream system failure, API change, or integration error reduced the volume of inputs reaching the model
questions.explain: A sudden 60% volume drop with stable per-prediction performance is more consistent with an upstream integration problem — a source system not sending data, an API change breaking the connection, or a pipeline failure — than with drift, user behavior, or monitoring issues. Drift typically degrades performance, not volume. The investigation starts with the data pipeline feeding the model.

questions.q: The project manager is designing a deployment plan for an AI model that will replace a high-stakes manual underwriting process. Which deployment approach BEST manages risk?
opts: Shadow deployment — AI runs in background without affecting decisions while the manual process continues
Full cutover — replace the manual process immediately to realize the efficiency gains quickly
Parallel run — operate AI alongside manual underwriting for a period, comparing outputs before full cutover
Staged rollout — deploy to a pilot business unit first, evaluate outcomes, then expand to all units
questions.explain: For a high-stakes process, a staged rollout (start with a pilot unit, evaluate outcomes, expand based on evidence) provides the best risk management while delivering real operational value. A parallel run also works but requires more resources. Shadow deployment doesn't generate real-world outcome feedback. Full cutover for a high-stakes manual process with no parallel validation is too risky.

questions.q: A regulatory change requires that all AI models used in financial decisions include an explanation mechanism for all affected consumers. The project manager discovers the current model cannot generate explanations. What is the MOST appropriate action?
opts: Continue operating while building explanatory capability in parallel over the next year
Immediately assess whether the regulation requires a compliance date and develop a remediation plan to meet it
Add a general disclosure statement that AI was used in the decision as a temporary compliance measure
Petition the regulator for an exemption based on the model's high accuracy and established business value
questions.explain: Regulatory compliance has a defined timeline — the first question is when compliance is required, then planning remediation to meet that date. Operating without compliance while building in parallel only works if the regulatory timeline permits it. General disclosure doesn't satisfy an explanation requirement. Seeking exemptions before assessing requirements inverts the process.

questions.q: A model serving infrastructure upgrade is planned. The operations team wants to use a blue-green deployment approach. The project manager should ensure this approach includes:
opts: A model performance validation on the new infrastructure before shifting live traffic, and a rollback plan if issues emerge
Approval from the data science team confirming the model will perform identically on the new infrastructure
Communication to all end users that a brief service interruption is planned during the infrastructure cutover
A cost analysis confirming the blue-green infrastructure cost is within the operational budget
questions.explain: Blue-green deployment for model infrastructure must include validation that the model performs identically on the new environment before live traffic is shifted — infrastructure differences can subtly change model behavior or latency. A rollback plan must exist in case issues emerge after traffic shift. Budget approval, data science sign-off, and user communication are secondary requirements.

questions.q: A project manager is establishing the handoff from the project team to the operations team for a deployed AI model. What is the MOST critical element to include in the handoff package?
opts: Project financial documentation showing all development costs and projected operational ROI
Meeting notes from all Phase I through Phase V project team retrospectives and lessons learned sessions
Complete source code for all model development experiments run during Phase III and Phase IV
Model card, monitoring plan, retraining procedure, incident response playbook, and escalation contacts
questions.explain: The operations team needs to manage the model without the project team's ongoing involvement. The critical package: model card (what the model does, its limitations, intended use), monitoring plan (what to watch), retraining procedure (when and how to retrain), incident response playbook (what to do when things go wrong), and escalation contacts. Source code, financials, and retrospectives serve other purposes.

questions.q: A post-deployment review shows the AI model delivered 40% of the ROI projected in Phase I. The gap is not due to technical failure. What should the project manager investigate?
opts: Whether a competitor deployed a similar model that has reduced the addressable value opportunity
Whether the model's accuracy has degraded since deployment, reducing its prediction quality
Whether the Phase I ROI assumptions about adoption rates, use case frequency, or value per prediction were inaccurate
Whether the MLOps team implemented the monitoring incorrectly, causing underreporting of model impact
questions.explain: If the model works technically but ROI is 40% of projection, the Phase I ROI assumptions were likely wrong — adoption was lower than expected, the use case was less frequent than assumed, or the value per correct prediction was overestimated. CPMAI requires investigating the specific assumption that failed, not blaming technical performance, monitoring, or external competition without evidence.

questions.q: An AI model that recommends investment products is producing outputs that are accurate but the compliance officer notes they sometimes recommend products outside the customer's risk profile. What action is required?
opts: Add a compliance disclaimer to all investment recommendations produced by the AI model
Implement a rule-based constraint layer that filters AI recommendations to only products within each customer's defined risk profile
Retrain the model with additional training examples showing only in-profile product recommendations
Raise the issue with the product team to update product risk profile definitions to better match AI outputs
questions.explain: An AI recommending products outside a customer's risk profile is a regulatory compliance and customer harm issue requiring immediate remediation. A rule-based constraint layer that filters outputs to compliant products is the most immediate and reliable fix — it applies compliance rules deterministically on top of AI outputs. Disclaimers don't prevent harm. Retraining takes time. Changing risk profiles to match AI is backwards.

questions.q: After production deployment, the model's average prediction confidence drops gradually over six weeks without any obvious single-event cause. What does this MOST likely indicate?
opts: Gradual data drift — the real-world input distribution is slowly diverging from the training distribution
A configuration change in the scoring pipeline that altered how features are preprocessed before scoring
Increased diversity in incoming queries causing the model to encounter more out-of-distribution examples
A memory leak in the model serving infrastructure degrading compute performance over time
questions.explain: Gradual, smooth decline in confidence scores over weeks without a discrete event is the characteristic signature of data drift — real-world inputs are slowly moving away from the training distribution. Sudden drops suggest infrastructure or configuration changes. Gradual drift is the expected pattern when business conditions, user behavior, or market dynamics change over time.

questions.q: A deployed AI model produces a critical error affecting 500 customer accounts. The operations team wants to retrain the model immediately to fix the issue. What should the project manager do FIRST?
opts: Notify the governance board and affected customers before taking any technical corrective action
Conduct a root cause analysis of the error before deciding on the appropriate corrective action
Authorize immediate retraining to minimize the duration of incorrect predictions in production
Activate the rollback plan to revert to the previous stable model while the error is investigated
questions.explain: When a deployed AI model causes harm, the first action is harm containment — rolling back to the previous stable model stops the error from affecting more customers. Root cause investigation is important but comes after harm is contained. Retraining without understanding the error may reproduce it. Governance notification and customer communication are important but follow rollback, not precede it.

questions.q: A project manager is conducting a 90-day post-deployment review. Adoption is strong but the business team has begun using the model for a purpose outside its original intended use. What should the project manager do?
opts: Notify the data science team to begin training a new model specifically for the emerging use case
Support the expanded use since strong adoption demonstrates the model's value across multiple use cases
Assess whether the new use case falls within the model's validated scope and require a formal use case extension review if it does not
Document the expanded use as an organizational success story demonstrating AI value creation
questions.explain: Using an AI model for purposes outside its validated scope creates reliability, fairness, and governance risks — the model may not perform appropriately for the new use case, bias testing may not have been done for it, and governance accountability may be unclear. CPMAI requires formal assessment and, if needed, a use case extension review before expanded use is approved.

questions.q: The organization is scaling an AI model from one business unit to five. The original deployment had 2 stakeholder groups. The scaled deployment will have 15. What risk should the project manager flag?
opts: The model may not be technically accurate enough for the additional business units' use cases
Stakeholder management complexity increases significantly — communications, requirements gathering, and change management must be scaled accordingly
The model serving infrastructure may not scale to the additional prediction volume across five business units
The MLOps team may not have sufficient capacity to support five simultaneous model deployments
questions.explain: Scaling from 2 to 15 stakeholder groups multiplies the stakeholder management complexity — each group may have different requirements, workflow integrations, governance expectations, and adoption challenges. The project manager must flag this as a significant change management and communications planning challenge, not just a technical scaling problem.

questions.q: A deployed AI model's business owner asks the project manager to disable monitoring for two weeks during a major system migration to reduce operational complexity. What should the project manager advise?
opts: Advise against disabling monitoring and propose maintaining it at reduced scope during the migration if resources are constrained
Agree but require daily manual performance checks as a compensating control during the monitoring gap
Escalate to the governance board since disabling monitoring requires formal risk acceptance at the appropriate level
Agree since monitoring can be restored quickly after the migration is complete with minimal risk
questions.explain: Disabling monitoring exposes the organization to undetected model failures for two weeks — especially during a system migration when integration changes can cause unexpected model behavior. CPMAI advises against disabling monitoring and proposes an alternative: maintaining monitoring at reduced scope (core metrics only) during the migration. If full disabling is considered, it requires formal governance risk acceptance, not bilateral agreement.

questions.q: An AI model produces a recommendation that a user flags as clearly incorrect. The user bypasses the recommendation and the correct outcome results. What process should the project manager ensure captures this event?
opts: A customer service escalation process that compensates the user for the incorrect recommendation
A model performance report updated to reflect the reduction in accuracy caused by the error event
A bug report filed with the development team for root cause analysis and model debugging
A feedback loop process that captures user overrides with outcome data to inform model improvement and monitoring
questions.explain: User overrides with known correct outcomes are valuable training signal and model quality feedback. CPMAI Phase V requires a structured feedback loop that captures these events, documents the override rationale, and feeds outcome data back into the model improvement cycle. Bug reports, compensation processes, and accuracy reports don't create the systematic feedback mechanism CPMAI requires.

questions.q: A model's monitoring alerts fire at a high rate, causing alert fatigue — the operations team has started ignoring them. What is the MOST important corrective action?
opts: Implement an automated response system that handles low-severity alerts without human intervention
Reduce the total number of monitoring metrics tracked to lower the overall alert volume
Review and recalibrate alert thresholds to ensure alerts fire only for genuinely actionable situations requiring human response
Assign a dedicated operations staff member to review all alerts and filter false positives before distribution
questions.explain: Alert fatigue from poorly calibrated thresholds is a serious monitoring failure — when teams ignore alerts, critical failures go undetected. The root cause is almost always thresholds set too sensitively, firing alerts that aren't truly actionable. Recalibrating thresholds to ensure alerts require and deserve human response is the correct fix. Reducing metrics, adding staff, or automating responses don't fix threshold miscalibration.

sections.title: AI Project Management: Scenarios & Judgment
sections.description: Cross-domain PM · Stakeholder management · Risk · Change control · Communication · Team dynamics

questions.q: A data scientist on the team believes the AI solution will eliminate their job function over time. This belief is affecting their engagement and work quality. What should the project manager do?
opts: Reassure the data scientist that AI will not eliminate their role and ask them to refocus on deliverables
Address the concern directly, clarify the team member's evolving role in the AI-enabled environment, and involve them in shaping how AI augments their function
Escalate to HR since job anxiety is a personnel issue outside the project manager's scope
Replace the team member with someone more supportive of AI transformation to protect project quality
questions.explain: A team member's fear about their own job security is a legitimate concern requiring direct, honest engagement — not dismissal or escalation. CPMAI project managers acknowledge the concern, clarify how roles evolve in an AI-augmented environment, and involve the team member in shaping the solution. Dismissing, escalating, or replacing avoids the real issue and undermines team trust.

questions.q: The project manager is preparing a lessons learned document at project closure. What element would be MOST valuable for the organization's future AI projects?
opts: Documented insights on data quality issues, estimation accuracy, stakeholder engagement approaches, and governance decisions that affected outcomes
A technical specification of the final model architecture and development environment configuration
A competency assessment of each team member's performance during the project for HR reference
A financial summary showing actual costs versus Phase I estimates across all five CPMAI phases
questions.explain: The most organizationally valuable lessons learned address the recurring challenges in AI projects: data quality problems and how they were identified and resolved, estimation patterns (where AI projects consistently differ from initial plans), what stakeholder engagement approaches worked, and which governance decisions were effective. Technical specifications and financial summaries serve narrow audiences.

questions.q: A project manager is closing an AI project. The model has been handed over to operations but the project team still receives escalations from the business because operations hasn't fully taken ownership. What should the project manager do?
opts: Reopen the project to provide continued support since the handoff was evidently premature
Document the incomplete handoff as a lessons learned item and close the project as planned
Continue handling escalations until the business is comfortable with the operations team's support capability
Formally complete the project closure, establish a defined transition period with clear handoff milestones, and redirect escalations to operations
questions.explain: An incomplete operational handoff requires a formal, structured transition — not continued project team support or immediate hard closure. A defined transition period with specific handoff milestones (operations handles tier-1 escalations by date X, full support by date Y) and a clear redirect protocol gives operations the time to mature while ending the project team's ambiguous support role.

questions.q: An AI project is in Phase IV evaluation and is on track. The organization announces a merger that will expand the customer base by 300%. What Phase I activity should the project manager revisit?
opts: The vendor contracts since the expanded customer base will require renegotiating licensing agreements
The project timeline since the merger will likely delay regulatory approval for deployment
The business case and scope — the merger changes the population the model will serve and may require revalidating feasibility and success criteria
The team composition since the merger may provide access to additional data science talent
questions.explain: A 300% customer base expansion fundamentally changes the use case — the model was designed for the original population. Phase I feasibility must be revisited: is the training data representative of the expanded population, are the success metrics still appropriate at this scale, and are the ethical and bias assessments valid for new customer segments? The business case scope has materially changed.

questions.q: After deployment, the project manager receives feedback that a specific user group finds the AI model's interface confusing and consistently misinterprets outputs. What is the MOST important first step?
opts: Retrain the model to produce outputs that match the user group's existing mental models
Improve the output presentation, explanation format, and user training for that specific group before assuming the model needs to change
Commission a user research study to determine how the AI model should be redesigned for broader usability
Increase monitoring for that user group to detect whether their misinterpretation leads to downstream errors
questions.explain: Confusing interface and misinterpreted outputs are most likely a UX and training problem, not a model problem. The first step is improving how outputs are presented and explained to that user group, and providing better training before concluding the model itself needs retraining. Retraining is an expensive intervention that addresses the model, not the communication gap.

questions.q: A business stakeholder repeatedly requests features outside the project's defined scope, causing the data science team to spend time evaluating and declining requests. What should the project manager do?
opts: Establish a formal change request process and communicate it to the stakeholder so requests are properly evaluated and documented
Escalate the stakeholder behavior to their manager as a project interference issue
Accept the most reasonable out-of-scope requests to maintain positive stakeholder relationships
Ask the data science team to ignore out-of-scope requests and continue focusing on approved deliverables
questions.explain: Recurring out-of-scope requests require a formal change request process — this gives the stakeholder a proper channel (their requests are heard and evaluated), gives the project manager visibility into all requests, and prevents informal scope creep from consuming team capacity. Ignoring requests damages the relationship; escalating is premature; accepting without process creates ungoverned scope expansion.

questions.q: The project manager is asked to estimate the effort for building a new AI feature. The team has no prior experience with this specific AI technique. What is the BEST estimation approach?
opts: Apply a 3x multiplier to the data science lead's initial estimate to account for AI project uncertainty
Defer the estimate until Phase II when data availability is confirmed and the technical approach is clearer
Use the average effort from similar features in the team's prior projects as the baseline estimate
Conduct a time-boxed spike experiment to gain actual experience with the technique before estimating
questions.explain: When the team has no experience with a specific AI technique, estimation without evidence is speculation. A time-boxed spike experiment — a short focused investigation — produces actual experience-based data to ground the estimate. Generic multipliers don't improve estimate quality. Prior project averages don't apply if the technique is different. Deferral delays the estimate without improving it.

questions.q: A project team member suggests that because the AI model makes thousands of decisions per day, occasional errors are statistically acceptable and don't require individual review. The project manager should:
opts: Disagree and implement manual review for every individual AI decision to eliminate harm from errors
Agree since statistical error tolerances are the appropriate standard for high-volume AI systems
Challenge this — individual errors may be acceptable statistically but still cause significant harm to the specific individuals affected, requiring a proportionate response process
Agree but establish a threshold below which individual errors are automatically closed without review
questions.explain: Statistical acceptability of error rates at scale does not eliminate the harm to individuals affected by each error. CPMAI's responsible AI principles require a proportionate response process — affected individuals need a way to report errors, have them reviewed, and be remediated. 'Statistically acceptable at scale' is not an ethical justification for having no individual recourse mechanism.

questions.q: A project team is highly technically skilled but has no experience with stakeholder management for AI projects. The project manager notices key business stakeholders are disengaged. What is the MOST appropriate action?
opts: Assign a business analyst to act as a translator between the technical team and business stakeholders
Develop and implement a stakeholder engagement plan that creates regular touchpoints and meaningful participation opportunities for business stakeholders
Reduce the frequency of business stakeholder meetings to allow the technical team to focus on development
Escalate stakeholder disengagement to the sponsor as a risk to project adoption
questions.explain: Stakeholder disengagement in an AI project threatens adoption and business alignment. The correct response is a structured stakeholder engagement plan — regular touchpoints, decision checkpoints that matter to business stakeholders, and participation opportunities that make engagement meaningful. A translator helps with communication but doesn't substitute for a genuine engagement strategy.

questions.q: Halfway through Phase III, the project manager discovers a new open-source AI library that would significantly improve model performance. Adding it would require refactoring 40% of the existing codebase. What should the project manager do?
opts: Evaluate the performance gain against the refactoring cost, risk, and timeline impact before making a recommendation
Defer the library to the next model iteration since mid-phase codebase changes are inherently too risky
Ask the data science team to implement the library in parallel and select whichever approach is ready first
Implement the library immediately since performance improvements are always worth the refactoring investment
questions.explain: A new library offering performance gains mid-phase requires trade-off analysis — how significant is the performance improvement, what is the refactoring cost, what is the timeline risk, and does the improvement materially affect business outcomes? Neither 'always worth it' nor 'always too risky' is the right default. CPMAI requires evidence-based evaluation before recommending course change.

questions.q: A project team is building an AI model for a use case that is similar to one that failed at the organization three years ago. What should the project manager do with this organizational history?
opts: Share the historical failure with the sponsor as context but proceed without letting past results affect the current approach
Assign the same team members who worked on the prior project to avoid repeating known mistakes
Treat the historical failure as irrelevant since three years have passed and technology has advanced significantly
Research the prior failure's root causes and specifically address them in the current project's risk management and feasibility assessment
questions.explain: Prior failures are organizational knowledge that should directly inform current project risk management. The project manager should research specifically why the prior project failed — data quality, stakeholder alignment, business case problems, or technical issues — and explicitly address those root causes in the current project's assessment and planning. Ignoring history or simply informing stakeholders without acting on it wastes the learning.

questions.q: An AI project team consists of data scientists, data engineers, and business analysts but does not include anyone with legal or compliance expertise. What risk should the project manager flag?
opts: The team's communication style may not meet the standards expected by executive stakeholders
The team lacks sufficient technical diversity to evaluate multiple algorithm approaches effectively
Legal and compliance gaps may cause the team to proceed with data use, model deployment, or automation that violates regulations
Without legal expertise, the team cannot protect intellectual property developed during the project
questions.explain: Missing legal and compliance expertise creates risk that the team will make data, modeling, or deployment decisions that violate regulations — without anyone to identify the violation. CPMAI requires cross-functional team composition to include the perspectives needed to identify all significant project risks, including legal and regulatory ones.

questions.q: A risk was identified in Phase I: 'The data required may not be available in sufficient volume.' Now in Phase III, the data shortage has materialized. What should the project manager have done differently?
opts: Escalated the risk immediately in Phase I rather than including it in the risk register
Defined risk response actions in Phase I and triggered those responses when early warning signs appeared in Phase II
Deferred the project until the data shortage risk was fully resolved before committing to any further phases
Added the risk to the steering committee report in Phase I to transfer ownership to the governance board
questions.explain: Identifying a risk in Phase I creates an obligation to define response actions — not just document it. The project manager should have planned risk responses in Phase I (e.g., alternative data sources, scope adjustment, data augmentation strategies) and monitored for early warning signals during Phase II. Risk register documentation without response planning is incomplete risk management.

questions.q: The project's AI governance board has not met in three months due to scheduling conflicts, causing decisions to accumulate. What should the project manager do?
opts: Escalate the governance gap to executive leadership and propose alternative decision mechanisms to clear the backlog
Defer all pending decisions until the governance board reconvenes and accept the resulting project delays
Identify which decisions are within the project manager's authority and make those, escalating only the highest-risk items
Make the pending decisions independently to maintain project momentum during the governance gap
questions.explain: A three-month governance gap with accumulated decisions is a systemic problem requiring escalation to executive leadership. The governance board needs either a meeting to clear the backlog or an interim decision mechanism. Making decisions independently beyond the PM's authority, deferring indefinitely, or selectively escalating are all inadequate responses to a governance process breakdown.

questions.q: A project manager realizes the AI project's success has created organizational dependency: the business cannot operate without the model if it fails. What governance control should be recommended?
opts: Document the dependency in the model card and monitor for any performance degradation that might affect business continuity
Commission a second identical AI model as a hot standby to eliminate the single point of failure risk
Develop a manual fallback procedure that the business can execute if the AI model becomes unavailable
Retrain the model more frequently to reduce the probability of performance failure causing business disruption
questions.explain: Critical business dependency on an AI model requires a manual fallback procedure — a defined process the business can execute if the model is unavailable due to failures, maintenance, or unexpected drift. More frequent retraining, documentation, and redundant models address different risks. A manual fallback addresses the unavailability scenario that creates the dependency risk.

questions.q: A project manager is managing an AI project where the data science team consistently underestimates experiment iteration times. After four sprints, actual time is 2.3x the estimates. What should the project manager do?
opts: Accept the estimation variance as inherent to AI project uncertainty and rebaseline the schedule accordingly
Apply a 2.3x correction factor to all future estimates automatically and update the project plan accordingly
Facilitate a team retrospective to understand why estimation is off and collaboratively improve estimation accuracy
Escalate to the sponsor that the team's estimation quality is causing project delays
questions.explain: Systematic 2.3x estimation error requires investigation — why are estimates consistently off? Is it undiscovered complexity, scope creep within iterations, or methodology issues? A team retrospective to diagnose and improve estimation is the correct CPMAI response. Applying a correction factor without understanding the cause perpetuates the problem. Escalating without improvement planning is premature.

questions.q: The project sponsor asks the project manager for weekly AI performance updates but the model monitoring system only produces meaningful data on a monthly basis. What should the project manager communicate?
opts: Provide weekly updates using daily manual samples since the sponsor's expectation must be met
Explain the monitoring system's data cadence and propose what meaningful weekly indicators can be shared versus what requires monthly review
Decline weekly updates entirely since providing them would mislead the sponsor with incomplete data
Change the monitoring system to produce weekly data to meet the sponsor's reporting needs
questions.explain: The project manager should explain what can meaningfully be reported weekly (deployment health, incident count, user adoption) versus what requires monthly aggregation (model performance metrics). Providing misleading weekly AI performance metrics is worse than no report. Declining entirely fails the stakeholder. Changing the monitoring system for reporting cadence alone may be unnecessary overhead.

questions.q: A senior data scientist disagrees with the project manager's Phase II data quality go/no-go decision. The data scientist believes the data is good enough to proceed. What should the project manager do?
opts: Defer to the data scientist since they have greater technical expertise to evaluate data quality
Document both perspectives, present the disagreement with evidence to the steering committee, and let the committee decide
Compromise by proceeding to Phase III with the understanding that data issues will be addressed during development
Override the data scientist since the project manager has final authority on phase gate decisions
questions.explain: A substantive disagreement between the project manager's governance assessment and a senior technical expert's recommendation should be escalated to the steering committee with both perspectives documented. Neither party should unilaterally override the other. Compromise by proceeding with known quality issues is not a sound governance decision — it defers the problem to a more expensive phase.

questions.q: A project manager learns that the organization is planning to use the AI model's outputs to train a new AI model without the knowledge of the customers whose data was used. What should the project manager flag?
opts: Whether the data science team has capacity to support both the current model and a new model development effort
Whether the new model would create competitive risks if the approach became known to market competitors
Whether the new AI model will be technically compatible with the existing data infrastructure
Whether using model outputs to train a new model complies with the original data consent under which customer data was collected
questions.explain: Using AI model outputs derived from customer data to train a new model may constitute secondary use of personal data beyond what customers consented to. CPMAI's responsible AI principles require evaluating consent compliance for all downstream uses of data — including derived outputs. Technical compatibility, team capacity, and competitive risk are secondary to the consent and legal use question.

questions.q: An AI project is 70% complete when a key data scientist resigns unexpectedly. What should the project manager do FIRST?
opts: Pause all development work until a replacement is hired to prevent knowledge gaps from causing errors
Immediately begin recruiting a replacement data scientist before assessing the full impact of the departure
Assess the knowledge transfer risk — what critical knowledge the departing data scientist holds and whether it is documented
Notify the project sponsor of the departure and request emergency budget for a replacement hire
questions.explain: The immediate risk from a key departure is undocumented knowledge — if the data scientist holds undocumented understanding of data decisions, model architecture choices, or experiment results, that knowledge leaves with them. The first action is assessing what they know that isn't documented and capturing it before they leave. Recruiting, notifying sponsors, and pausing work are important but secondary to knowledge preservation.
